question: Do the original definition of 'representative' and the 2020-amended definition of 'persons associated with investment advice' cover the same set of persons within an investment advisory firm?
answer: No
explanation: The original definition of 'representative' was narrow: only employees or agents who directly render investment advice on behalf of the investment adviser were covered. The 2020-amended definition of 'persons associated with investment advice' is significantly broader: it includes members, partners, officers, directors, employees, and all client-facing sales staff — regardless of whether they directly render investment advice — as long as they are engaged in investment advisory services. The Explanation also deems all client relationship managers and service relationship managers as persons associated with investment advice. Only purely clerical/administrative staff with no client interface are excluded. The scope of the 2020 definition is materially wider, covering persons (e.g., sales staff, directors) who were not covered by the original 'representative' definition.

context: Regulation 10 (original, 2013): The certificate of registration granted under regulation 9 shall be valid for a period of five years from the date of its issue. [Amended by SEBI (Change in Conditions of Registration of Certain Intermediaries) (Amendment) Regulations, 2016, w.e.f. 08-12-2016]: The certificate of registration granted under regulation 9 shall be valid till it is suspended or cancelled by the Board.
question: What was the validity period of an investment adviser's certificate of registration under the original SEBI (Investment Advisers) Regulations, 2013, and when was it changed to perpetual validity?
answer: Under the original 2013 regulations, a certificate of registration was valid for a period of five years from the date of its issue and required renewal thereafter. This was changed to perpetual validity (valid till suspended or cancelled by the Board) by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Change in Conditions of Registration of Certain Intermediaries) (Amendment) Regulations, 2016, which came into force on 8 December 2016.

context: Regulation 8 (Networth, pre-2024): (1) Investment advisers who are non-individuals shall have a net worth of not less than fifty lakh rupees. (2) Investment advisers who are individuals shall have net tangible assets of value not less than five lakh rupees: Provided that existing investment advisers shall comply with the networth requirement within three years from the date of commencement of the SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Amendment) Regulations, 2020. [Substituted as 'Deposit' by SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2024, w.e.f. 16-12-2024.]
question: What was the networth requirement for non-individual investment advisers under the SEBI (Investment Advisers) Regulations, 2013 before the 2024 amendment substituted it with a 'deposit' requirement?
answer: Before the substitution by the SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2024 (w.e.f. 16-12-2024), Regulation 8 required non-individual investment advisers to have a net worth of not less than fifty lakh rupees. Individual investment advisers were required to have net tangible assets of not less than five lakh rupees. The 2024 amendment replaced these specific numerical thresholds with a 'deposit' of such sum as specified by the Board from time to time.

context: Regulation 7(1)(a) (pre-2020 Amendment): A professional qualification or graduate degree or post-graduate degree or post graduate diploma (minimum two years in duration) in finance, accountancy, business management, commerce, economics, capital market, banking, insurance or actuarial science or other financial services as may be specified from a university or an institution recognized by the Central Government or any State Government or a recognised foreign university or institution or association or a professional qualification by completing a Post Graduate Program in the Securities Market (Investment Advisory) from NISM of a duration not less than one year or a professional qualification by obtaining a CFA Charter from the CFA Institute. [As substituted w.e.f. 30-09-2020 and further updated w.e.f. 25-11-2025]: A graduate degree or any equivalent educational qualification from a recognized institution or CFA Charter, and relevant certification from NISM or accredited body.
question: How did the minimum qualification requirement for individual investment advisers under SEBI IA Regulations change with the 2020 amendment, and what further modification was made in 2025?
answer: Under the original 2013 regulations, Regulation 7(1)(a) required a professional qualification or PG degree/diploma of minimum two years duration specifically in finance, accountancy, business management, commerce, economics, capital markets, banking, insurance, or actuarial science — or a CFA Charter. The SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Amendment) Regulations, 2020 (w.e.f. 30-09-2020) broadened this by permitting any professional qualification or graduate/post-graduate degree or diploma with no restriction on field of study, alongside NISM certification. The SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2025 (w.e.f. 25-11-2025) further updated Regulation 7(1)(a) to require only a graduate degree (any discipline) or CFA Charter plus relevant NISM certification — removing the old requirement for a finance-specific discipline or work experience.

context: Regulation 22 (original, 2013): Investment advisers which are banks, NBFCs and body corporate providing distribution or execution services to their clients shall keep their investment advisory services segregated from such activities: Provided that such distribution or execution services can only be offered subject to the following: (a) The client shall not be under any obligation to avail the distribution or execution services offered by the investment adviser. (b) The investment adviser shall maintain arms length relationship. (c) All fees and charges paid to distribution or execution service providers by the client shall be paid directly to the service providers and not through the investment adviser. [Substituted by SEBI (IA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2020, w.e.f. 30-09-2020 to require: individual IAs cannot provide distribution services; non-individual IAs to have client-level segregation at group level.]
question: What was the original scope of Regulation 22 in the SEBI (Investment Advisers) Regulations, 2013, and how did the 2020 amendment fundamentally change its coverage?
answer: The original Regulation 22 (2013) was titled 'Segregation of execution services' and applied only to investment advisers that are banks, NBFCs, and body corporates which also provide distribution or execution services. Such entities could continue offering distribution services alongside advice provided they maintained operational segregation, ensured clients were not obligated to avail distribution services, maintained arms-length relationships, and ensured fees were paid directly to service providers. Individual investment advisers were not explicitly covered. The SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Amendment) Regulations, 2020 (w.e.f. 30-09-2020) completely substituted Regulation 22 — retitling it 'Client level segregation of advisory and distribution activities.' The 2020 version imposed a complete prohibition on individual investment advisers (and their families) from providing distribution services, and replaced the prior operational-segregation approach for non-individual entities with a stricter client-level segregation at the group level.

context: Regulation 21(1) (original, pre-2023): An investment adviser shall redress client grievances promptly. [As amended by SEBI (Facilitation of Grievance Redressal Mechanism) (Amendment) Regulations, 2023, w.e.f. 18-08-2023]: The Investment Adviser shall redress investor grievances promptly but not later than twenty-one calendar days from the date of receipt of the grievance and in such manner as may be specified by the Board.
question: When was the specific 21-calendar-day deadline for redressing investor grievances introduced for investment advisers, and what did the regulation previously require?
answer: The 21-calendar-day deadline for redressing investor grievances was introduced by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Facilitation of Grievance Redressal Mechanism) (Amendment) Regulations, 2023, with effect from 18 August 2023. Prior to this amendment, Regulation 21(1) of the SEBI (Investment Advisers) Regulations, 2013 only required that an investment adviser 'redress client grievances promptly' — a qualitative standard with no specific numerical time limit.

context: Regulation 13(e) (as amended w.e.f. 25-11-2025 by SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2025): individuals registered as investment advisers whose number of clients exceed three hundred at any point of time or the fee collected during the financial year exceeds three crore rupees, whichever is earlier shall — (i) immediately intimate the Administration and Supervisory Body and initiate the process of transition from individual to non-individual investment adviser; and (ii) apply for grant of in-principle approval for registration as non-individual investment adviser within 3 months from the date on which the threshold is reached. [Prior to this amendment, per the 2020 version]: individuals... shall — (i) apply for grant of in-principle registration as non-individual investment adviser; (ii) the in-principle registration shall be valid for a period of three months.
question: What procedural change did the SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2025 make to the transition process for individual investment advisers who breach the 300-client or Rs 3 crore fee threshold?
answer: The SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2025 (w.e.f. 25-11-2025) amended Regulation 13(e) to add a new intermediate step: upon breaching the threshold, the individual IA must first immediately intimate the Administration and Supervisory Body and initiate the transition process before applying for in-principle approval. The 2020 version of Regulation 13(e) only required directly applying for in-principle registration as a non-individual investment adviser. The 3-month window for applying for in-principle approval was retained by the 2025 amendment, but an explicit intimation obligation was added as a new first step.

context: Regulation 13(h) (inserted by SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2024, w.e.f. 16-12-2024): the number of clients of a part-time investment adviser shall not exceed seventy-five in total at any point of time. Regulation 2(1)(qa) (inserted w.e.f. 16-12-2024): 'part-time investment adviser' means an individual or a firm, who for consideration, is engaged in the business of providing investment advice and is also engaged in any other business activity or employment.
question: When was the category of 'part-time investment adviser' and the 75-client limit introduced under SEBI IA Regulations, 2013, and what is the definition of a part-time investment adviser?
answer: The concept of 'part-time investment adviser' and the 75-client limit were both introduced by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2024, with effect from 16 December 2024. A 'part-time investment adviser' is defined under Regulation 2(1)(qa) as an individual or a firm who, for consideration, is engaged in the business of providing investment advice and is also engaged in any other business activity or employment. Prior to this amendment, there was no regulatory distinction between full-time and part-time investment advisers.

context: Regulation 15(14) (inserted by SEBI (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2024, w.e.f. 16-12-2024): An investment adviser who uses Artificial Intelligence tools, irrespective of the scale and scenario of adoption of such tools, for servicing its clients shall be solely responsible for the security, confidentiality, integrity of the client data, use of any other information or data to arrive at investment advice, investment advice based on output of Artificial Intelligence tools and compliance with any law for the time being in force.
question: When was the regulatory obligation regarding the use of Artificial Intelligence tools by investment advisers first introduced under SEBI IA Regulations, 2013, and what does it require?
answer: The obligation regarding the use of Artificial Intelligence tools was first introduced by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Investment Advisers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2024, with effect from 16 December 2024 (Regulation 15(14)). It requires that any investment adviser using AI tools for servicing clients — irrespective of the scale or nature of AI adoption — shall be solely responsible for: the security, confidentiality, and integrity of client data; any other information or data used to arrive at investment advice; investment advice based on the output of AI tools; and compliance with all applicable laws.

context: Regulation 3(1): On and from the commencement of these regulations, no entity or person shall act as an Alternative Investment Fund unless it has obtained a certificate of registration from the Board.
question: Can an entity in India operate as an Alternative Investment Fund without obtaining a certificate of registration from SEBI under the SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012?
answer: No. Under Regulation 3(1), no entity or person shall act as an Alternative Investment Fund on and from the commencement of these regulations unless it has obtained a certificate of registration from the Board (SEBI).

context: Regulation 3(7): Without prejudice to the powers of the Board to take any action under the Act or regulations made thereunder, the certificate of registration shall be valid till the Alternative Investment Fund is wound up.
question: For how long is a certificate of registration granted to an Alternative Investment Fund under SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 valid?
answer: Under Regulation 3(7), the certificate of registration is valid until the Alternative Investment Fund is wound up. The certificate does not have a fixed time-bound expiry — it remains in force for the entire operational life of the AIF.

context: Regulation 6(4): The Board may, on being satisfied that the applicant complies with the provisions of regulation 4 except those of clause (c) or clause (d) thereof, as the case may be, grant an in-principle approval to the applicant: Provided that the applicant shall comply with clause (c) or clause (d) of regulation 4, as the case may be, within six months from the date of grant of in-principle approval and upon compliance with the same, the Board may grant a certificate of registration. (5) An Alternative Investment Fund that has been granted in-principle approval may accept commitments from investors but shall not accept any monies till it is granted registration under sub-regulation (2) of this regulation.
question: Under SEBI AIF Regulations 2012, what can an AIF that has received in-principle approval (but not yet full registration) do in terms of fundraising, and what is it prohibited from doing?
answer: Under Regulation 6(5), an AIF that has been granted in-principle approval may accept commitments from investors. However, it shall not accept any monies (actual cash) until it is granted the full certificate of registration. The AIF must also comply with any remaining eligibility conditions (under Reg 4(c) or (d)) within six months from the date of in-principle approval to obtain full registration.

context: Regulation 3(4): Alternative Investment Funds shall seek registration in one of the categories mentioned hereunder — (a) 'Category I AIF' which invests in start-up or early stage ventures or social ventures or SMEs or infrastructure or other sectors which the government or regulators consider as socially or economically desirable — includes venture capital funds, SME Funds, angel funds, social impact funds, infrastructure funds, special situation funds; (b) 'Category II AIF' which does not fall in Category I and III and which does not undertake leverage or borrowing other than as permitted in these regulations — includes private equity funds or debt funds; (c) 'Category III AIF' which employs diverse or complex trading strategies and may employ leverage including through investment in listed or unlisted derivatives — includes hedge funds.
question: What are the three categories of Alternative Investment Funds under SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012, and what is the key distinguishing characteristic of each?
answer: Under Regulation 3(4): Category I AIFs invest in start-ups, early stage ventures, social ventures, SMEs, infrastructure, or sectors considered socially/economically desirable — includes venture capital funds, SME funds, angel funds, social impact funds, and infrastructure funds. Category II AIFs are those that do not fall in Category I or III and do not undertake leverage beyond what is permitted in these regulations — includes private equity funds and debt funds. Category III AIFs employ diverse or complex trading strategies and may employ leverage through listed or unlisted derivatives — includes hedge funds.

context: Regulation 10(b): each scheme of the Alternative Investment Fund shall have corpus of at least twenty crore rupees: Provided that each scheme of the social impact fund shall have a corpus of at least five crore rupees.
question: What is the minimum corpus requirement for each scheme of an Alternative Investment Fund under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012? Does this requirement differ for social impact funds?
answer: Under Regulation 10(b), each scheme of an AIF must have a corpus of at least Rs twenty crore. However, for social impact funds, each scheme must have a corpus of at least Rs five crore — a reduced threshold recognizing the social purpose of such funds.

context: Regulation 10(c): the Alternative Investment Fund shall not accept from an investor, an investment of value less than one crore rupees: Provided that in case of investors who are employees or directors of the Alternative Investment Fund or employees or directors of the Manager, the minimum value of investment shall be twenty five lakh rupees: Provided further that this clause shall not apply to an accredited investor.
question: What is the minimum investment that an investor must make in an Alternative Investment Fund scheme under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012? Are there any exceptions to this minimum?
answer: Under Regulation 10(c), the minimum investment by any investor in an AIF scheme is Rs one crore. Two exceptions apply: (1) employees or directors of the AIF, or employees or directors of the Manager, have a reduced minimum of Rs twenty-five lakh; (2) the minimum investment requirement does not apply at all to accredited investors.

context: Regulation 10(d): the Manager or Sponsor shall have a continuing interest in the Alternative Investment Fund of not less than two and half percent of the corpus or five crore rupees, whichever is lower, in the form of investment in the Alternative Investment Fund and such interest shall not be through the waiver of management fees: Provided that for Category III Alternative Investment Fund, the continuing interest shall be not less than five percent of the corpus or ten crore rupees, whichever is lower.
question: What continuing interest is the Manager or Sponsor of an Alternative Investment Fund required to maintain under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012? Does the requirement differ between Category I/II and Category III funds?
answer: Under Regulation 10(d), the Manager or Sponsor must maintain a continuing interest of not less than 2.5% of the corpus or Rs 5 crore, whichever is lower, in the form of investment in the AIF (not via waiver of management fees). For Category III AIFs, the threshold is higher: not less than 5% of the corpus or Rs 10 crore, whichever is lower.

context: Regulation 10(f): no scheme of the Alternative Investment Fund shall have more than one thousand investors: Provided that accredited investors shall be excluded while computing the number of investors in a scheme of an Alternative Investment Fund.
question: What is the maximum number of investors permitted in a single scheme of an Alternative Investment Fund under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012? Are accredited investors counted toward this limit?
answer: Under Regulation 10(f), no scheme of an AIF shall have more than one thousand investors. Accredited investors are explicitly excluded from the computation of this investor count — meaning the 1,000-investor cap applies only to non-accredited investors.

context: Regulation 9(2): Any material alteration to the fund strategy shall be made with the consent of atleast two-thirds of unit holders by value of their investment in the Alternative Investment Fund.
question: What level of investor consent is required to make a material alteration to an Alternative Investment Fund's strategy under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: Under Regulation 9(2), any material alteration to the fund strategy requires the consent of at least two-thirds of unit holders by value of their investment in the Alternative Investment Fund.

context: Regulation 12(2): Such placement memorandum shall be filed with the Board through a merchant banker atleast thirty days prior to launch of scheme along with the fees as specified in the Second Schedule: Provided that payment of scheme fees shall not apply in case of launch of first scheme by the Alternative Investment Fund.
question: What are the requirements for filing a placement memorandum before launching a new scheme under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012? Is there any fee exemption?
answer: Under Regulation 12(2), a placement memorandum must be filed with the Board through a merchant banker at least thirty days prior to the launch of the scheme, along with the fees as specified in the Second Schedule. There is one fee exemption: the payment of scheme fees does not apply in case of the launch of the first scheme by an Alternative Investment Fund.

context: Regulation 13(1): Category I Alternative Investment Fund and Category II Alternative Investment Fund shall be close ended and the tenure of fund or scheme shall be determined at the time of application subject to sub-regulation (2) of this regulation. (2) Category I and II Alternative Investment Fund or schemes launched by such funds shall have a minimum tenure of three years. (3) Schemes of Category III Alternative Investment Fund may be open ended or close ended.
question: What are the structural and tenure requirements applicable to Category I and II Alternative Investment Funds, and how do they differ from Category III AIFs?
answer: Under Regulations 13(1) and (2), Category I and II AIFs must be close-ended and the tenure of the fund or scheme must be determined at the time of application; such funds or their schemes must have a minimum tenure of three years. Under Regulation 13(3), schemes of Category III AIFs have no such restriction — they may be open-ended or close-ended.

context: Regulation 13(5): Extension of the tenure of the close ended Alternative Investment Fund may be permitted up to two years subject to approval of two-thirds of the unit holders by value of their investment in the Alternative Investment Fund: Provided that an Accredited Investors only fund may be permitted to extend its tenure up to five years subject to the approval of two-thirds of the unit holders by value of their investment in the Accredited Investors only fund.
question: Under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012, by how many years can the tenure of a close-ended AIF be extended, and what investor approval is required? Is there a special provision for Accredited Investors only funds?
answer: Under Regulation 13(5), the tenure of a close-ended AIF may be extended by up to two years, subject to the approval of two-thirds of unit holders by value of their investment. For Accredited Investors only funds, there is a special provision — their tenure may be extended by up to five years (instead of two), subject to the same approval threshold of two-thirds of unit holders by value.

context: Regulation 14(1): Units of close ended Alternative Investment Fund may be listed on stock exchange subject to a minimum tradable lot of one crore rupees. (2) Listing of Alternative Investment Fund units shall be permitted only after final close of the fund or scheme.
question: What are the conditions under which the units of a close-ended AIF may be listed on a stock exchange under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: Under Regulation 14, units of a close-ended AIF may be listed on a stock exchange subject to two conditions: (1) the minimum tradable lot must be Rs one crore; and (2) listing is permitted only after the final close of the fund or scheme. Listing is not permitted prior to the final close.

context: Regulation 15(1)(c): Category I and II of Alternative Investment Funds shall invest not more than twenty five per cent of the investable funds in an Investee Company directly or through investment in the units of other Alternative Investment Funds: Provided that large value funds for accredited investors of Category I and II may invest up to fifty percent of the investable funds in an investee company directly or through investment in the units of other Alternative Investment Funds.
question: What is the single-investee concentration limit for Category I and II Alternative Investment Funds under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012? Does this limit differ for large value funds for accredited investors?
answer: Under Regulation 15(1)(c), Category I and II AIFs may invest not more than 25% of their investable funds in any single investee company (directly or through other AIF units). Large value funds for accredited investors in Category I and II may invest up to 50% of investable funds in a single investee company.

context: Regulation 15(1)(d): Category III Alternative Investment Funds shall invest not more than ten per cent of the investable funds in an Investee Company, directly or through investment in units of other Alternative Investment Funds and the large value funds for accredited investors of Category III Alternative Investment Funds may invest up to twenty per cent of the investable funds in an Investee Company, directly or through investment in units of other Alternative Investment Funds.
question: What is the single-investee concentration limit for Category III Alternative Investment Funds under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: Under Regulation 15(1)(d), Category III AIFs may invest not more than 10% of their investable funds in any single investee company (directly or through other AIF units). Large value funds for accredited investors in Category III may invest up to 20% of investable funds in a single investee company.

context: Regulation 15(1)(e): Alternative Investment Fund shall not invest except with the approval of seventy five percent of investors by value of their investment in the Alternative Investment Fund in — (a) associates; or (b) units of Alternative Investment Funds managed or sponsored by its Manager, Sponsor or associates of its Manager or Sponsor.
question: Under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012, what investor approval is required before an AIF can invest in its associate companies or in units of other AIFs managed by the same manager or sponsor?
answer: Under Regulation 15(1)(e), an AIF requires the approval of 75% of investors by value of their investment before investing in: (a) its associates, or (b) units of AIFs managed or sponsored by its Manager, Sponsor, or their associates.

context: Regulation 16(1)(c): Category I Alternative Investment Funds shall not borrow funds directly or indirectly or engage in any leverage for the purpose of making investments or otherwise, except for borrowing funds to meet temporary funding requirements and day-to-day operational requirements for not more than thirty days, on not more than four occasions in a year and not more than ten percent of the investable funds.
question: Under what conditions may a Category I Alternative Investment Fund borrow funds under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012? What are the specific restrictions on such borrowing?
answer: Under Regulation 16(1)(c), Category I AIFs shall not borrow funds or engage in leverage for investment purposes. The only permitted borrowing is for temporary funding requirements and day-to-day operational requirements, subject to three cumulative conditions: (1) borrowing shall not be for more than thirty days at a time; (2) borrowing shall not occur on more than four occasions in a year; and (3) borrowing shall not exceed ten percent of the investable funds.

context: Regulation 16(2)(a): at least seventy-five percent of the investable funds shall be invested in unlisted equity shares or equity linked instruments of a venture capital undertaking or in companies listed or proposed to be listed on a SME exchange or SME segment of an exchange: Provided that the investment conditions specified in clause (a) shall be achieved by the fund by the end of its life cycle.
question: What minimum percentage of investable funds must a venture capital fund (which is a sub-category of Category I AIF) invest in qualifying instruments under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: Under Regulation 16(2)(a), at least 75% of the investable funds of a venture capital fund must be invested in unlisted equity shares or equity-linked instruments of a venture capital undertaking, or in companies listed or proposed to be listed on an SME exchange or SME segment of an exchange. This 75% threshold must be achieved by the fund by the end of its life cycle.

context: Regulation 10(d): the Manager or Sponsor shall have a continuing interest in the Alternative Investment Fund of not less than two and half percent of the corpus or five crore rupees, whichever is lower, in the form of investment in the Alternative Investment Fund.
question: A Category I AIF has a total corpus of Rs 300 crore. What is the minimum continuing interest the Manager or Sponsor must maintain in this fund under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: The continuing interest requirement is the lower of: (a) 2.5% of corpus = 2.5% × Rs 300 crore = Rs 7.5 crore, or (b) Rs 5 crore. Since Rs 5 crore < Rs 7.5 crore, the minimum continuing interest is Rs 5 crore. The Manager/Sponsor must maintain at least Rs 5 crore of investment in the fund.

context: Regulation 10(d): Provided that for Category III Alternative Investment Fund, the continuing interest shall be not less than five percent of the corpus or ten crore rupees, whichever is lower.
question: A Category III AIF (hedge fund) has a corpus of Rs 150 crore. What is the minimum continuing interest the Manager or Sponsor is required to maintain in this fund?
answer: For Category III AIFs, the continuing interest must be the lower of: (a) 5% of corpus = 5% × Rs 150 crore = Rs 7.5 crore, or (b) Rs 10 crore. Since Rs 7.5 crore < Rs 10 crore, the minimum continuing interest is Rs 7.5 crore.

context: Regulation 10(b): each scheme of the Alternative Investment Fund shall have corpus of at least twenty crore rupees: Provided that each scheme of the social impact fund shall have a corpus of at least five crore rupees.
question: An AIF Manager is planning to launch three schemes: Scheme A (private equity fund), Scheme B (social impact fund), and Scheme C (infrastructure fund). What is the minimum total corpus that must be raised across all three schemes to comply with SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: Scheme A (private equity, Category II): minimum Rs 20 crore. Scheme B (social impact fund, Category I): minimum Rs 5 crore. Scheme C (infrastructure fund, Category I): minimum Rs 20 crore. Total minimum corpus = Rs 20 crore + Rs 5 crore + Rs 20 crore = Rs 45 crore.

context: Regulation 10(c): the Alternative Investment Fund shall not accept from an investor, an investment of value less than one crore rupees: Provided that in case of investors who are employees or directors of the Alternative Investment Fund or employees or directors of the Manager, the minimum value of investment shall be twenty five lakh rupees.
question: A Category II AIF has 4 investors: an external institutional investor, a director of the Manager, an employee of the AIF, and an accredited investor. Their proposed investments are Rs 75 lakh, Rs 20 lakh, Rs 30 lakh, and Rs 50 lakh respectively. Which investments, if any, fail to meet the minimum investment requirements under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: External institutional investor (Rs 75 lakh): fails the minimum of Rs 1 crore. Director of the Manager (Rs 20 lakh): fails the reduced minimum of Rs 25 lakh applicable to manager's directors. Employee of AIF (Rs 30 lakh): meets the Rs 25 lakh minimum for AIF employees. Accredited investor (Rs 50 lakh): no minimum applies — Regulation 10(c) does not apply to accredited investors. Therefore, two investments fail: the external institutional investor (Rs 75 lakh < Rs 1 crore) and the director of the Manager (Rs 20 lakh < Rs 25 lakh).

context: Regulation 12(2): Such placement memorandum shall be filed with the Board through a merchant banker atleast thirty days prior to launch of scheme along with the fees as specified in the Second Schedule.
question: An AIF Manager plans to launch a new scheme on October 15, 2025. By what date must the placement memorandum be filed with SEBI under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: The placement memorandum must be filed at least 30 days prior to the launch. Launch date: October 15, 2025. Subtracting 30 days: October 15 − 30 days = September 15, 2025. The placement memorandum must be filed by September 15, 2025 at the latest.

context: Regulation 2(1)(ab): 'accredited investor' means any person who is granted a certificate of accreditation by an accreditation agency who — (ii) in case of a body corporate, has net worth of at least fifty crore rupees.
question: A body corporate has a net worth of Rs 45 crore. Can it qualify as an accredited investor under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012? By how much does it fall short of the accredited investor threshold?
answer: No. Under Regulation 2(1)(ab)(ii), a body corporate must have a net worth of at least Rs 50 crore to qualify as an accredited investor. With a net worth of Rs 45 crore, the body corporate falls short by Rs 5 crore (Rs 50 crore − Rs 45 crore = Rs 5 crore). It does not qualify as an accredited investor.

context: Regulation 15(1)(c): Category I and II of Alternative Investment Funds shall invest not more than twenty five per cent of the investable funds in an Investee Company directly or through investment in the units of other Alternative Investment Funds.
question: A Category II AIF has investable funds of Rs 400 crore. The Manager wants to invest Rs 120 crore in Company X. Is this investment permissible? What is the maximum amount that can be invested in Company X?
answer: Maximum permitted investment in any single investee company = 25% of investable funds = 25% × Rs 400 crore = Rs 100 crore. The proposed investment of Rs 120 crore exceeds the limit by Rs 20 crore. The investment is not permissible as proposed. The maximum permissible investment in Company X is Rs 100 crore.

context: Regulation 13(2): Category I and II Alternative Investment Fund or schemes launched by such funds shall have a minimum tenure of three years. Regulation 13(5): Extension of the tenure of the close ended Alternative Investment Fund may be permitted up to two years subject to approval of two-thirds of the unit holders by value of their investment in the Alternative Investment Fund: Provided that an Accredited Investors only fund may be permitted to extend its tenure up to five years.
question: A Category II AIF was registered with a scheme tenure of 5 years. The tenure has now expired and unit holders are considering an extension. (a) What is the maximum total tenure (including extension) for a regular Category II AIF? (b) What would it be if the fund were an Accredited Investors only fund?
answer: (a) Regular Category II AIF: Original tenure = 5 years + maximum extension = 2 years (subject to 2/3 unit holder approval). Maximum total tenure = 7 years. (b) Accredited Investors only fund: Original tenure = 5 years + maximum extension = 5 years (subject to 2/3 unit holder approval). Maximum total tenure = 10 years. Note: the minimum tenure of 3 years (Reg 13(2)) is only a floor; the actual tenure is as determined at registration.

regulation_a: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — original Regulation 16(2)(a) on venture capital fund investment minimum (pre-2021)
regulation_b: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — Regulation 16(2)(a) as substituted w.e.f. 13-08-2021
context_a: Regulation 16(2)(a) (pre-2021): at least two-thirds of the investable funds shall be invested in unlisted equity shares or equity linked instruments of a venture capital undertaking or in companies listed or proposed to be listed on a SME exchange or SME segment of an exchange.
context_b: Regulation 16(2)(a) (as substituted w.e.f. 13-08-2021): at least seventy-five percent of the investable funds shall be invested in unlisted equity shares or equity linked instruments of a venture capital undertaking or in companies listed or proposed to be listed on a SME exchange or SME segment of an exchange: Provided that the investment conditions specified in clause (a) shall be achieved by the fund by the end of its life cycle.
question: Do the original and the 2021-amended versions of Regulation 16(2)(a) impose the same minimum investment requirement on venture capital funds in qualifying instruments?
answer: No
explanation: The original version required at least two-thirds (approximately 66.67%) of investable funds to be invested in qualifying venture capital/SME instruments. The 2021-amended version raised this minimum to seventy-five percent (75%). These are contradictory requirements — the original imposed a lower threshold (2/3) while the current version imposes a higher threshold (3/4). The 2021 amendment also added an explicit proviso that this threshold must be achieved by the end of the fund's life cycle.

regulation_a: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — Regulation 16(1)(c) before the 2024 amendment
regulation_b: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — Regulation 16(1)(c) as substituted w.e.f. 07-08-2024
context_a: Regulation 16(1)(c) (pre-2024): Category I Alternative Investment Funds shall not borrow funds directly or indirectly or engage in any leverage except for meeting temporary funding requirements for not more than thirty days, on not more than four occasions in a year and not more than ten percent of the investable funds.
context_b: Regulation 16(1)(c) (as substituted w.e.f. 07-08-2024): Category I Alternative Investment Funds shall not borrow funds directly or indirectly or engage in any leverage for the purpose of making investments or otherwise, except for borrowing funds to meet temporary funding requirements and day-to-day operational requirements for not more than thirty days, on not more than four occasions in a year and not more than ten percent of the investable funds.
question: Do the pre-2024 and post-2024 versions of Regulation 16(1)(c) permit the same scope of borrowing by Category I AIFs?
answer: No
explanation: The pre-2024 version only permitted borrowing for 'temporary funding requirements.' The 2024-amended version expanded the permitted purpose to include both 'temporary funding requirements' and 'day-to-day operational requirements.' The quantitative limits (30 days, 4 occasions per year, 10% of investable funds) remained the same. The key difference is that the 2024 version explicitly permits borrowing for day-to-day operational needs — a purpose not covered by the original version's narrower 'temporary funding' carveout.

regulation_a: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — definition of 'large value fund for accredited investors' under Regulation 2(1)(pa) before November 2025 amendment
regulation_b: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — definition under Regulation 2(1)(pa) as amended w.e.f. 19-11-2025
context_a: Regulation 2(1)(pa) (before November 2025 amendment): 'large value fund for accredited investors' means an Alternative Investment Fund or scheme of an Alternative Investment Fund in which each investor (other than the Manager, Sponsor, employees or directors of the Alternative Investment Fund or employees or directors of the Manager) is an accredited investor and invests not less than seventy crore rupees.
context_b: Regulation 2(1)(pa) (as amended w.e.f. 19-11-2025): 'large value fund for accredited investors' means an Alternative Investment Fund or scheme of an Alternative Investment Fund in which each investor (other than the Manager, Sponsor, employees or directors of the Alternative Investment Fund or employees or directors of the Manager) is an accredited investor and invests not less than twenty-five crore rupees.
question: Does the original definition of 'large value fund for accredited investors' and the November 2025-amended definition impose the same minimum investment threshold per investor?
answer: No
explanation: The original definition required each qualifying investor to invest not less than Rs seventy crore per investor. The November 2025-amended definition reduced this minimum to not less than Rs twenty-five crore per investor — a reduction of Rs 45 crore (nearly two-thirds reduction). These are directly contradictory thresholds: the pre-2025 version set a much higher investment bar of Rs 70 crore, while the current version lowers it to Rs 25 crore.

regulation_a: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — Regulation 15(1)(e) before the 2021 amendment
regulation_b: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — Regulation 15(1)(e) as substituted w.e.f. 05-05-2021
context_a: Regulation 15(1)(e) (pre-2021): Alternative Investment Fund shall not invest in associates except with the approval of seventy five percent of investors by value of their investment in the Alternative Investment Fund.
context_b: Regulation 15(1)(e) (as substituted w.e.f. 05-05-2021): Alternative Investment Fund shall not invest except with the approval of seventy five percent of investors by value of their investment in the Alternative Investment Fund in — (a) associates; or (b) units of Alternative Investment Funds managed or sponsored by its Manager, Sponsor or associates of its Manager or Sponsor.
question: Do the original and 2021-amended versions of Regulation 15(1)(e) cover the same categories of investments requiring 75% investor approval?
answer: No
explanation: The original version required 75% investor approval only for investment in associates. The 2021-amended version expanded this restriction to also cover investments in units of AIFs managed or sponsored by the Manager, Sponsor, or associates of the Manager or Sponsor. The original provision covered only a narrower class (associates), while the amended version adds a second category (affiliated AIF units) that was not previously subject to the 75% approval requirement — a material expansion of the restriction.

regulation_a: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — Regulation 13(5) on tenure extension for all close-ended AIFs (general provision, pre-2021)
regulation_b: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — Regulation 13(5) as amended with special provision for Accredited Investors only funds (post-2021)
context_a: Regulation 13(5) (pre-2021): Extension of the tenure of the close ended Alternative Investment Fund may be permitted up to two years subject to approval of two-thirds of the unit holders by value of their investment in the Alternative Investment Fund.
context_b: Regulation 13(5) (as amended): Extension of the tenure of the close ended Alternative Investment Fund may be permitted up to two years subject to approval of two-thirds of the unit holders by value of their investment in the Alternative Investment Fund: Provided that an Accredited Investors only fund may be permitted to extend its tenure up to five years subject to the approval of two-thirds of the unit holders by value of their investment in the Accredited Investors only fund.
question: Do these two versions of Regulation 13(5) prescribe the same maximum tenure extension period for all close-ended AIFs?
answer: No
explanation: The original version prescribed a single uniform maximum extension of two years for all close-ended AIFs. The amended version retains the two-year maximum for regular close-ended AIFs but introduces a special higher limit of five years for Accredited Investors only funds (subject to the same 2/3 unit holder approval). The two versions are contradictory as to the maximum extension period available to a fund that qualifies as an Accredited Investors only fund — the original permitted only 2 years while the amended version permits up to 5 years for such funds.

regulation_a: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — original eligibility criteria for key investment team under Regulation 4(g) (pre-2020)
regulation_b: SEBI (AIF) Regulations, 2012 — Regulation 4(g) as substituted w.e.f. 19-10-2020
context_a: Regulation 4(g) (pre-2020): the key investment team of the Manager of Alternative Investment Fund has adequate experience, with at least one key personnel having not less than five years experience in advising or managing pools of capital or in fund or asset or wealth or portfolio management or in the business of buying, selling and dealing of securities or other financial assets and has relevant professional qualification.
context_b: Regulation 4(g) (as substituted w.e.f. 19-10-2020): The key investment team of the Manager of Alternative Investment Fund has — (i) at least one key personnel with relevant certification as may be specified by the Board from time to time; and (ii) at least one key personnel with professional qualification in finance, accountancy, business management, commerce, economics, capital market or banking from a recognized university, or a CFA charter from the CFA institute or any other qualification as may be specified by the Board. [Both requirements may be fulfilled by the same key personnel.]
question: Do the original and 2020-amended versions of Regulation 4(g) impose the same eligibility requirements on the key investment team of an AIF Manager?
answer: No
explanation: The original version required the key investment team to include at least one key personnel with five or more years of experience in fund/portfolio management or securities dealing, combined with a relevant professional qualification — it was primarily experience-based. The 2020-amended version replaced this with a dual requirement: (i) at least one key personnel with a SEBI-specified certification, and (ii) at least one key personnel with a finance-related professional qualification or CFA Charter. The 2020 version no longer requires a minimum years-of-experience threshold — it replaced the experience requirement with a certification requirement. These are contradictory: one is experience-driven, the other is certification-driven.

context: Regulation 16(2)(a) (original): at least two-thirds of the investable funds shall be invested in unlisted equity shares or equity linked instruments of a venture capital undertaking or in companies listed or proposed to be listed on a SME exchange. [Substituted w.e.f. 13-08-2021 by SEBI (AIF) (Fourth Amendment) Regulations, 2018]: at least seventy-five percent of the investable funds shall be invested in such instruments.
question: When was the minimum investment threshold for venture capital funds (in qualifying venture capital/SME instruments) raised from two-thirds to seventy-five percent of investable funds under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: The minimum investment threshold for venture capital funds was raised from two-thirds (approximately 66.67%) to seventy-five percent of investable funds by the SEBI (Alternative Investment Funds) (Fourth Amendment) Regulations, 2018, which came into effect on 13 August 2021. Prior to this amendment, the threshold was at least two-thirds of investable funds.

context: Regulation 2(1)(pa) (inserted by SEBI (AIF) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2021, w.e.f. 03-08-2021): 'large value fund for accredited investors' means an Alternative Investment Fund or scheme in which each investor is an accredited investor and invests not less than seventy crore rupees. [Renamed to 'Accredited Investors only fund' and updated by SEBI (AIF) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2025, w.e.f. 19-11-2025.]
question: When was the concept of 'large value fund for accredited investors' first introduced under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012, and what was the original minimum investment threshold?
answer: The concept of 'large value fund for accredited investors' was first introduced by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Alternative Investment Funds) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2021, with effect from 3 August 2021. The original minimum investment per qualifying investor was not less than Rs seventy crore. This threshold was later reduced to Rs twenty-five crore by the SEBI (AIF) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2025, w.e.f. 19-11-2025.

context: Regulation 15(1)(e) (pre-2021): AIF shall not invest in associates except with approval of 75% of investors. [Substituted w.e.f. 05-05-2021 by SEBI (AIF) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2021]: AIF shall not invest except with approval of 75% of investors in — (a) associates; or (b) units of AIFs managed or sponsored by its Manager, Sponsor or associates of its Manager or Sponsor.
question: What change did the SEBI (AIF) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2021 make to the scope of related-party investment restrictions under Regulation 15(1)(e)?
answer: The SEBI (Alternative Investment Funds) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2021 (w.e.f. 05-05-2021) expanded the scope of Regulation 15(1)(e). Before the amendment, the 75% investor approval requirement applied only to investments in associates. The 2021 amendment added a second category: the approval requirement was extended to also cover investments in units of Alternative Investment Funds managed or sponsored by the Manager, Sponsor, or their associates — a category of related-party investment not previously subject to the 75% consent requirement.

context: Regulation 4(g) (original, pre-2020): the key investment team of the Manager of AIF has adequate experience, with at least one key personnel having not less than five years experience in advising or managing pools of capital or in fund or asset or wealth or portfolio management or in the business of buying, selling and dealing of securities or other financial assets and has relevant professional qualification. [Substituted by SEBI (AIF) (Amendment) Regulations, 2020, w.e.f. 19-10-2020]: team has at least one key personnel with relevant NISM certification AND at least one with professional qualification in finance or CFA Charter.
question: How did the SEBI (AIF) (Amendment) Regulations, 2020 change the eligibility requirements for the key investment team of an AIF Manager, and what was the significance of this change?
answer: The SEBI (Alternative Investment Funds) (Amendment) Regulations, 2020 (w.e.f. 19-10-2020) substituted Regulation 4(g) to replace the experience-based standard with a certification-based standard. The original requirement was for at least one key personnel to have not less than five years of experience in managing pools of capital or securities plus a relevant professional qualification. The 2020 amendment replaced this with two separate requirements: (i) at least one key personnel must hold a relevant SEBI-specified certification (not a minimum years-of-experience requirement), and (ii) at least one key personnel must hold a finance-related professional qualification or CFA Charter. The same person may fulfill both requirements. The key significance is the shift from experience as the primary criterion to certification as the primary requirement.

context: Regulation 2(1)(ga) (inserted by SEBI (AIF) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2023, w.e.f. 15-06-2023): 'Corporate Debt Market Development Fund' means an Alternative Investment Fund set up and making investments in terms of Chapter III-C of these regulations.
question: When was the Corporate Debt Market Development Fund (CDMDF) category introduced under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012?
answer: The Corporate Debt Market Development Fund was introduced by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Alternative Investment Funds) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2023, with effect from 15 June 2023. The CDMDF is defined as an Alternative Investment Fund set up and making investments in accordance with Chapter III-C of the AIF Regulations.

context: Regulation 3(4)(a) previously included 'social venture funds' as a sub-category of Category I AIF. [The term 'social venture funds' was substituted by 'social impact funds' by the SEBI (AIF) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2022, w.e.f. 25-07-2022.] The Explanation to Regulation 16(4) was simultaneously updated from 'social venture funds' to 'social impact funds'.
question: What was the name of the social-purpose AIF sub-category under Category I before it was renamed in 2022, and what was the new name?
answer: Before 25 July 2022, the sub-category was called 'social venture funds' — a term under Regulation 3(4)(a) for Category I AIFs investing primarily in securities or units of social ventures. The SEBI (Alternative Investment Funds) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2022 (w.e.f. 25-07-2022) renamed this sub-category to 'social impact funds.' Simultaneously, the definition of 'social venture fund' at Regulation 2(1)(v) was omitted and replaced with new definitions of 'social enterprise,' 'social impact fund,' 'social stock exchange,' and 'social units.'

context: Regulation 10(f) (as amended by SEBI (AIF) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2025, w.e.f. 19-11-2025): no scheme of the Alternative Investment Fund shall have more than one thousand investors: Provided that accredited investors shall be excluded while computing the number of investors in a scheme of an Alternative Investment Fund. [Prior to this amendment, all investors including accredited investors were counted toward the 1,000 cap.]
question: What change did the SEBI (AIF) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2025 make to how the 1,000-investor cap is calculated for an AIF scheme?
answer: The SEBI (Alternative Investment Funds) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2025 (w.e.f. 19-11-2025) amended Regulation 10(f) to exclude accredited investors from the computation of the 1,000-investor cap. Prior to this amendment, all investors (including accredited investors) were counted toward the 1,000-investor limit. After the 2025 amendment, accredited investors are not counted — only non-accredited investors are subject to the 1,000-investor ceiling. This effectively allows AIF schemes to have more than 1,000 total investors provided the excess consists of accredited investors.

context: Regulation 15(1)(i) (inserted by SEBI (AIF) (Amendment) Regulations, 2024, w.e.f. 05-01-2024): Alternative Investment Funds shall hold their investments in dematerialised form, subject to such conditions as may be specified by the Board from time to time: Provided that the requirement shall not apply to: (a) investments not eligible for dematerialisation, (b) investments held by a liquidation scheme that are not available in dematerialised form, and (c) such other investments as may be specified by the Board from time to time.
question: When was the requirement for AIFs to hold investments in dematerialised form introduced under SEBI AIF Regulations, 2012, and what exceptions apply?
answer: The requirement for AIFs to hold investments in dematerialised form was introduced by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Alternative Investment Funds) (Amendment) Regulations, 2024, with effect from 5 January 2024 (Regulation 15(1)(i)). Three exceptions apply: (a) investments in instruments that are not eligible for dematerialisation; (b) investments held by a liquidation scheme that are not available in dematerialised form; and (c) such other investments and schemes as may be specified by the Board from time to time.

context: Regulation 4: The Board shall not consider an application under regulation 3 unless the applicant is promoted by a person belonging to any of the following categories: (a) a public financial institution; (b) a scheduled commercial bank included in the second schedule to the RBI Act, 1934; (c) a foreign bank operating in India with the approval of the Reserve Bank of India; (d) a foreign credit rating agency incorporated in a Financial Action Task Force (FATF) member jurisdiction and recognised under their law, having a minimum of five years' experience in rating securities; (e) any company or body corporate having continuous net worth of minimum rupees one hundred crores as per audited annual accounts for the previous five years.
question: Who are the eligible categories of promoters for a credit rating agency under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: Under Regulation 4, a credit rating agency must be promoted by one of the following: (a) a public financial institution; (b) a scheduled commercial bank in the RBI's second schedule; (c) a foreign bank operating in India with RBI approval; (d) a foreign CRA incorporated in a FATF member jurisdiction, recognised under their law, with at least five years' experience in rating securities; or (e) any company or body corporate with a continuous net worth of at least Rs 100 crore per audited accounts for the previous five years.

context: Regulation 5(a)–(c): The Board shall not consider an application unless — (a) the applicant is set up and registered as a company under the Companies Act, 2013; (b) the applicant has, in its Memorandum of Association, specified rating activity as one of its main objects; (c) the applicant has a minimum net worth of rupees twenty five crore.
question: What are the minimum eligibility criteria relating to corporate form, business objects, and net worth for a credit rating agency applicant under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: Under Regulation 5, a CRA applicant must satisfy three minimum requirements: (a) it must be set up and registered as a company under the Companies Act, 2013; (b) its Memorandum of Association must specify rating activity as one of its main objects; and (c) it must have a minimum net worth of Rs twenty-five crore.

context: Regulation 8(2): The certificate of registration granted under sub-regulation (1) shall be valid unless it is suspended or cancelled by the Board.
question: How long is a certificate of registration granted to a credit rating agency under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 valid?
answer: Under Regulation 8(2), the certificate of registration is valid unless it is suspended or cancelled by the Board — it has no fixed expiry date.

context: Regulation 9(d): the credit rating agency shall at all times maintain a minimum net worth of rupees twenty five crore. Provided that a credit rating agency already registered with the Board having a net worth less than rupees twenty five crores, shall increase its net worth to the specified amount within a period of three years from the date of notification of the SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018.
question: What ongoing net worth requirement must a credit rating agency maintain at all times under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999? What transitional relief was provided for existing CRAs?
answer: Under Regulation 9(d), a credit rating agency must at all times maintain a minimum net worth of Rs twenty-five crore. Existing CRAs that were already registered and had a net worth below Rs twenty-five crore at the time of the 2018 amendment were given transitional relief — they must increase their net worth to the Rs 25 crore minimum within three years from the date of notification of the SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018.

context: Regulation 9(e): the promoter of the credit rating agency, in terms of regulation 4, shall maintain a minimum shareholding of 26% in the credit rating agency for a minimum period of three years from the date of grant of registration by the Board.
question: What shareholding requirement must the promoter of a credit rating agency maintain under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999, and for how long?
answer: Under Regulation 9(e), the promoter of a credit rating agency must maintain a minimum shareholding of 26% in the credit rating agency for a minimum period of three years from the date of grant of registration by the Board.

context: Regulation 9(f): a credit rating agency shall not carry out any activity other than the rating of securities that are listed or proposed to be listed on a stock exchange recognized by the Board. Nothing contained in these regulations shall preclude a credit rating agency from carrying out any other activity as may be specified by the Board or carrying out rating of financial instruments under the purview of any other financial sector regulator or authority as may be specified by the Board.
question: What restrictions apply to the activities a credit rating agency may undertake under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: Under Regulation 9(f), a CRA shall not carry out any activity other than the rating of securities that are listed or proposed to be listed on a stock exchange recognized by SEBI. Two exceptions apply: (1) the CRA may carry out any other activity as may be specified by the Board; and (2) the CRA may carry out rating of financial instruments under the purview of any other financial sector regulator or authority as specified by the Board.

context: Regulation 14: Every credit rating agency shall enter into a written agreement with each client whose securities it proposes to rate, and every such agreement shall include — (a) the rights and liabilities of each party; (b) the fee to be charged; (c) the client shall co-operate for periodic review; (d) the client shall provide true, adequate and timely information; (e) the CRA shall disclose the rating assigned to the client's securities irrespective of whether the rating is accepted by the client; (f) the client shall disclose all ratings assigned in the last three years and any unaccepted rating.
question: What must a written agreement between a credit rating agency and its client contain under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: Under Regulation 14, the written agreement must include: (a) the rights and liabilities of each party; (b) the fee to be charged; (c) an obligation on the client to co-operate for periodic review of the rating during the tenure of the rated instrument; (d) an obligation on the client to provide true, adequate, and timely information; (e) an obligation on the CRA to disclose the rating assigned to the client's securities through regular methods of dissemination, even if the rating is not accepted by the client; and (f) an obligation on the client to disclose in the offer document all ratings assigned in the last three years and any ratings by other CRAs not accepted by the client.

context: Regulation 15(2): Every credit rating agency shall disseminate information regarding newly assigned ratings, and changes in earlier rating promptly through press releases and websites, and, in the case of securities issued by listed companies, such information shall also be provided simultaneously to the concerned regional stock exchange and to all the stock exchanges where the said securities are listed.
question: How must a credit rating agency disseminate information about newly assigned ratings and rating changes under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999? What additional step is required for listed company securities?
answer: Under Regulation 15(2), a CRA must promptly disseminate information about newly assigned ratings and changes in earlier ratings through press releases and websites. For securities issued by listed companies, the information must additionally be provided simultaneously to the concerned regional stock exchange and to all stock exchanges where the securities are listed.

context: Regulation 16(2): If the client does not co-operate with the credit rating agency so as to enable the credit rating agency to comply with its obligations under regulation 15, the credit rating agency shall carry out the review on the basis of the best available information or in the manner as specified by the Board from time to time. Provided that if owing to such lack of co-operation, a rating has been based on the best available information, the credit rating agency shall disclose to the investors the fact that the rating is so based. (3) A credit rating agency shall not withdraw a rating so long as the obligations under the security rated by it are outstanding, except where the company whose security is rated is wound up or merged or amalgamated with another company.
question: What must a credit rating agency do if a client fails to cooperate during the rating review process under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999? Can a CRA withdraw a rating while the security's obligations are outstanding?
answer: Under Regulation 16(2), if a client fails to cooperate, the CRA must still carry out the review on the basis of best available information (or as specified by the Board), and must disclose to investors that the rating is based on best available information due to lack of client cooperation. Under Regulation 16(3), a CRA may not withdraw a rating so long as the obligations under the rated security are outstanding — withdrawal is only permitted if the rated company is wound up, or merged/amalgamated with another company (or as otherwise specified by the Board).

context: Regulation 18: (1) Every credit rating agency — (a) shall make public the definitions of the concerned rating, along with the symbol and, (b) shall also state that the ratings do not constitute recommendations to buy, hold or sell any securities. (2) Every credit rating agency shall make available to the general public information relating to the rationale of the ratings, which shall cover an analysis of the various factors justifying a favourable assessment, as well as factors constituting a risk.
question: What disclosure obligations about rating definitions and rationale must a credit rating agency comply with under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: Under Regulation 18, a CRA must: (1) make public the definitions of the concerned rating with the symbol, and must state that ratings do not constitute recommendations to buy, hold, or sell any securities; and (2) make available to the general public information on the rationale of its ratings, which must cover both factors justifying a favourable assessment and factors constituting a risk.

context: Regulation 21(1): Every credit rating agency shall keep and maintain, for a minimum period of five years, the following books of accounts, records and documents: (a) copy of its balance sheet; (b) a copy of its profit and loss account; (c) a copy of the auditor's report; (d) a copy of the agreement entered into with each client; (e) information supplied by each client; (f) correspondence with each client; (g) ratings assigned, including upgradation and downgradation; (h) rating notes considered by the rating committee; (i) record of decisions of the rating committee; (j) letter assigning rating; (k) particulars of fees charged.
question: For how long and what types of records must a credit rating agency maintain under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: Under Regulation 21(1), a CRA must maintain all records for a minimum period of five years. The required records include: balance sheets, profit and loss accounts, auditor's reports, client agreements, information and correspondence from clients, ratings assigned (including upgrades and downgrades), rating notes and committee decisions, rating assignment letters, and fee particulars.

context: Regulation 22: Every credit rating agency shall, within two month's from the date of the auditor's report, take steps to rectify the deficiencies if any, made out in the auditor's report, insofar as they relate to the activity of rating of securities.
question: Within what time period must a credit rating agency rectify deficiencies identified in the auditor's report under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: Under Regulation 22, a credit rating agency must take steps to rectify any deficiencies identified in the auditor's report (insofar as they relate to the rating of securities activity) within two months from the date of the auditor's report.

context: Regulation 24(4): All rating decisions, including the decisions regarding changes in rating, shall be taken by the rating committee. (8) A credit rating agency shall not rate securities issued by it.
question: Who has the authority to take rating decisions (including rating changes) under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999? Can a CRA rate its own securities?
answer: Under Regulation 24(4), all rating decisions — including decisions on changes in rating — must be taken by the rating committee. Individual analysts or management cannot unilaterally assign or change ratings. Under Regulation 24(8), a CRA is explicitly prohibited from rating securities issued by itself.

context: Regulation 24A(1): A credit rating agency shall not: (a) directly or indirectly, hold 10 per cent or more shareholding and/or voting rights in any other credit rating agency, or (b) have representation on the Board of any other credit rating agency. (2) A shareholder holding 10 per cent or more shares and/or voting rights in a credit rating agency shall not hold 10 per cent or more shares and/or voting rights, directly or indirectly, in any other credit rating agency. Provided that the said restriction shall not apply to holdings by Pension Funds, Insurance Schemes and Mutual Fund Schemes.
question: What shareholding restrictions apply between credit rating agencies under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999? Are there any exemptions?
answer: Under Regulation 24A, a CRA may not directly or indirectly hold 10% or more shareholding or voting rights in any other CRA, and may not have board representation in any other CRA. Additionally, any shareholder holding 10% or more in one CRA may not hold 10% or more (directly or indirectly) in any other CRA. There is an exemption: these cross-shareholding restrictions do not apply to holdings by Pension Funds, Insurance Schemes, and Mutual Fund Schemes.

context: Regulation 26(1): No credit rating agency shall rate a security issued by its promoter. (2) In case promoter is a lending institution, its Chairman, director or employee shall not be a Chairman, director or employee of credit rating agency or its rating committee.
question: Under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999, can a credit rating agency rate the securities of its own promoter? What additional restriction applies when the promoter is a lending institution?
answer: No. Under Regulation 26(1), a CRA is prohibited from rating any security issued by its promoter. Under Regulation 26(2), if the promoter is a lending institution, there is an additional structural restriction — no Chairman, director, or employee of the lending institution-promoter may simultaneously be a Chairman, director, or employee of the credit rating agency or its rating committee.

context: Regulation 27(1): No credit rating agency shall rate a security issued by an entity which is — (a) a borrower of its promoter; or (b) a subsidiary of its promoter; or (c) an associate of its promoter, if there are common Chairman, Directors between credit rating agency and these entities, or common employees, or common Chairman, Directors, Employees on the rating committee. (2) No credit rating agency shall rate a security issued by its associate or subsidiary, if the credit rating agency or its rating committee has a Chairman, director or employee who is also a Chairman, director or employee of any such entity.
question: What conflict-of-interest restrictions apply to the rating of securities of promoter-connected entities under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: Under Regulation 27(1), a CRA shall not rate securities of: (a) a borrower of its promoter; (b) a subsidiary of its promoter; or (c) an associate of its promoter — if there are common Chairmen, directors, or employees between the CRA and such entities, or common persons on the rating committee. Under Regulation 27(2), a CRA shall also not rate securities of its own associate or subsidiary if the CRA or its rating committee shares a Chairman, director, or employee with such entities.

context: Regulation 28C: On and from the date of this regulation coming into force, no person shall act as an ESG rating provider unless it has obtained a certificate from the Board: Provided that a person acting as an ESG Rating Provider on the date of this regulation coming into force, may continue to do so for a period of six months from the date of this regulation coming into force or such other period as may be specified by the Board, or if it has made an application for grant of a certificate for registration within the specified period, till the disposal of such application.
question: Under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 (as amended), can an entity provide ESG ratings without registration? What transitional period was provided to existing ESG rating providers?
answer: No. Under Regulation 28C, no person may act as an ESG rating provider without obtaining a certificate from the Board. Existing ESG rating providers operating before the regulation came into force were provided a transitional period of six months (or such other period as specified by the Board) to continue operations. If they filed an application for registration within the six-month period, they could continue until the disposal of their application.

context: Regulation 28E(m)(iv): [Category I ESG rating provider] the applicant shall maintain a minimum liquid net worth of rupees five crores at all times: Provided that at the time of making the application, the applicant shall have a minimum liquid net worth of the higher of, A. rupees ten crores, or B. addition of rupees five crores and the target on cumulative cash losses until breakeven.
question: What are the ongoing and application-time liquid net worth requirements for a Category I ESG rating provider under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: Under Regulation 28E(m)(iv), a Category I ESG rating provider must maintain a minimum liquid net worth of Rs 5 crore at all times on an ongoing basis. At the time of making the application for registration, the applicant must have a minimum liquid net worth equal to the higher of: (A) Rs 10 crore, or (B) Rs 5 crore plus the applicant's own projected target of cumulative cash losses until its targeted breakeven date.

context: Regulation 9(d): the credit rating agency shall at all times maintain a minimum net worth of rupees twenty five crore. Provided that a credit rating agency already registered with the Board having a net worth less than rupees twenty five crores, shall increase its net worth to the specified amount within a period of three years from the date of notification of the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Credit Rating Agencies) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018.
question: A credit rating agency registered with SEBI had a net worth of Rs 18 crore at the time the 2018 Amendment Regulations were notified. By how much must it increase its net worth to comply with the requirement, and by when?
answer: The minimum required net worth is Rs 25 crore. The CRA's current net worth is Rs 18 crore. Required increase = Rs 25 crore − Rs 18 crore = Rs 7 crore. The CRA must increase its net worth by Rs 7 crore within three years from the date of notification of the SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018.

context: Regulation 4(d): a foreign credit rating agency incorporated in a Financial Action Task Force (FATF) member jurisdiction and recognised under their law, having a minimum of five years' experience in rating securities.
question: A foreign credit rating agency was incorporated in its home country in January 2018 and began actively rating securities in its home jurisdiction from July 2019. It applies to promote a new CRA in India in December 2025. Does it meet the 'minimum of five years' experience in rating securities' requirement under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: The foreign CRA began rating securities from July 2019. By December 2025, it has had 6 years and 5 months of rating experience (July 2019 to December 2025). This exceeds the minimum five-year experience requirement. Yes, the foreign CRA meets the five-year experience requirement.

context: Regulation 22: Every credit rating agency shall, within two month's from the date of the auditor's report, take steps to rectify the deficiencies if any, made out in the auditor's report, insofar as they relate to the activity of rating of securities.
question: An auditor submits a report to a CRA on October 3, 2025, identifying deficiencies in its rating activities. What is the deadline by which the CRA must take steps to rectify these deficiencies under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: The CRA must take steps to rectify the deficiencies within two months from the date of the auditor's report. Date of report: October 3, 2025. Adding two months: December 3, 2025. The deadline for rectification is December 3, 2025.

context: Regulation 9(e): the promoter of the credit rating agency, in terms of regulation 4, shall maintain a minimum shareholding of 26% in the credit rating agency for a minimum period of three years from the date of grant of registration by the Board.
question: A CRA's promoter holds 35% of the CRA's shares at the time of registration. The CRA was granted registration on April 1, 2023. Can the promoter legally reduce its shareholding to 20% on June 15, 2026?
answer: The promoter must maintain at least 26% shareholding for a minimum of three years from the date of grant of registration. Three years from April 1, 2023 = April 1, 2026. June 15, 2026 is after April 1, 2026, so the lock-in period of three years has expired by then. After April 1, 2026, the promoter is no longer bound by the 3-year lock-in. However, reducing to 20% would breach the minimum 26% threshold if this obligation is ongoing beyond the three-year period (which the regulation's wording 'for a minimum period of three years' suggests is a lock-in, not a permanent requirement). The regulation specifies a minimum period of three years; after that, the 26% floor from Regulation 9(e) no longer applies as a mandatory minimum. Therefore, the promoter can reduce its shareholding to 20% on June 15, 2026.

context: Regulation 28E(m)(iv): [Category I ESG rating provider] maintain a minimum liquid net worth of rupees five crores at all times: at the time of making the application, the applicant shall have a minimum liquid net worth of the higher of, A. rupees ten crores, or B. addition of rupees five crores and the target on cumulative cash losses until breakeven.
question: A Category I ESG rating provider applicant projects cumulative cash losses of Rs 6 crore until breakeven. What is the minimum liquid net worth the applicant must demonstrate at the time of filing the registration application?
answer: The minimum liquid net worth at application time is the higher of: (A) Rs 10 crore, or (B) Rs 5 crore + projected cumulative cash losses until breakeven = Rs 5 crore + Rs 6 crore = Rs 11 crore.
