Since Rs 11 crore > Rs 10 crore, the minimum required liquid net worth at application time is Rs 11 crore.

context: Regulation 28E(n)(i): [Category II ESG rating provider] maintain minimum liquid net worth of rupees ten lakh at all times: at the time of making the application, the applicant shall have a minimum liquid net worth of the higher of, A. rupees twenty lakhs; or B. addition of rupees ten lakhs and the target on cumulative cash losses until breakeven.
question: A Category II ESG rating provider applicant projects cumulative cash losses of Rs 8 lakh until breakeven. What is the minimum liquid net worth at application time, and how does it compare to the ongoing maintenance requirement?
answer: At application time, the minimum is the higher of: (A) Rs 20 lakh, or (B) Rs 10 lakh + Rs 8 lakh (projected losses) = Rs 18 lakh. Since Rs 20 lakh > Rs 18 lakh, the minimum liquid net worth at application time is Rs 20 lakh. The ongoing maintenance requirement is Rs 10 lakh. Therefore, the application-time requirement (Rs 20 lakh) is double the ongoing maintenance requirement (Rs 10 lakh).

context: Regulation 24A(2): A shareholder holding 10 per cent or more shares and/or voting rights in a credit rating agency shall not hold 10 per cent or more shares and/or voting rights, directly or indirectly, in any other credit rating agency. Provided that the said restriction shall not apply to holdings by Pension Funds, Insurance Schemes and Mutual Fund Schemes.
question: Institution X holds 12% of the shares of CRA-1. It proposes to acquire a 9% stake in CRA-2. Would this acquisition violate Regulation 24A(2) of the SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: No, the proposed acquisition would not violate Regulation 24A(2). The restriction applies to a shareholder holding 10% or more in one CRA acquiring 10% or more in another CRA. Institution X already holds 12% (≥10%) in CRA-1. However, the proposed acquisition in CRA-2 is only 9%, which is below the 10% threshold. Since the combined holding in CRA-2 (9%) is less than 10%, the restriction under Regulation 24A(2) is not triggered. The acquisition is permissible.

context: Regulation 5(e): any company or body corporate, having continuous net worth of minimum rupees one hundred crores as per its audited annual accounts for the previous five years prior to filing of the application with the Board for the grant of certificate.
question: A body corporate files an application to promote a credit rating agency in December 2025. Its audited net worth for the preceding years was: 2020-21: Rs 95 crore; 2021-22: Rs 102 crore; 2022-23: Rs 110 crore; 2023-24: Rs 108 crore; 2024-25: Rs 115 crore. Does it meet the Regulation 4(e) promoter eligibility criterion?
answer: The requirement under Regulation 4(e) is a continuous net worth of at least Rs 100 crore per audited annual accounts for the previous five years. The five years preceding December 2025 would be FY 2020-21 through FY 2024-25. Net worth history: FY 2020-21: Rs 95 crore (fails — below Rs 100 crore), FY 2021-22: Rs 102 crore, FY 2022-23: Rs 110 crore, FY 2023-24: Rs 108 crore, FY 2024-25: Rs 115 crore. Since FY 2020-21 (Rs 95 crore) is below the Rs 100 crore threshold, the 'continuous' net worth requirement is not met. The body corporate does not meet the eligibility criterion under Regulation 4(e).

regulation_a: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — original Regulation 8(2) certificate validity (original version at commencement)
regulation_b: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — Regulation 8(2) as amended by SEBI (Change in Conditions of Registration of Certain Intermediaries) (Amendment) Regulations, 2016, w.e.f. 08-12-2016
context_a: Regulation 8(2) (pre-2016, as inserted by 2011 Amendment): The certificate of initial registration granted under sub-regulation (1) shall be valid for a period of five years from the date of its issue to the applicant.
context_b: Regulation 8(2) (as substituted w.e.f. 08-12-2016): The certificate of registration granted under sub-regulation (1) shall be valid unless it is suspended or cancelled by the Board.
question: Do these two versions of Regulation 8(2) impose the same validity period for a credit rating agency's certificate of registration?
answer: No
explanation: The pre-2016 version (inserted by the 2011 amendment) prescribed a fixed five-year validity period for the certificate of initial registration. The 2016-amended version abolished the fixed time limit — the certificate is now valid in perpetuity unless the Board suspends or cancels it. The original version was time-bound (5 years) requiring renewal or re-registration, while the amended version grants indefinite validity.

regulation_a: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — original Regulation 5(c) on minimum net worth (pre-2018)
regulation_b: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — Regulation 5(c) as substituted by SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 30-05-2018
context_a: Regulation 5(c) (original, pre-2018): the applicant has a minimum net worth of rupees five crores.
context_b: Regulation 5(c) (as substituted w.e.f. 30-05-2018): the applicant has a minimum net worth of rupees twenty five crore.
question: Do the original and 2018-amended versions of Regulation 5(c) prescribe the same minimum net worth requirement for credit rating agency applicants?
answer: No
explanation: The original Regulation 5(c) prescribed a minimum net worth of Rs five crore. The 2018-amended version raised this to Rs twenty-five crore — a five-fold increase. These are directly contradictory: the original set a much lower threshold of Rs 5 crore, while the current version requires Rs 25 crore. This represents a significant tightening of the financial eligibility criteria for CRA registration.

regulation_a: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — original Regulation 14(g) on mandatory dual rating (pre-2011 Second Amendment)
regulation_b: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — Regulation 14(g) as substituted by SEBI (CRA) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2011, w.e.f. 27-12-2011
context_a: Regulation 14(g) (original): the client shall agree to obtain a rating from at least two different rating agencies for any issue of debt securities whose size is equal to or exceeds, rupees one hundred crores.
context_b: Regulation 14(g) (as substituted w.e.f. 27-12-2011): the client shall obtain a rating for any issue of debt securities in accordance with the relevant regulations.
question: Do the original Regulation 14(g) and the 2011-amended version impose the same mandatory rating requirement for large debt security issues?
answer: No
explanation: The original Regulation 14(g) imposed a mandatory dual-rating requirement — for any debt issue of Rs 100 crore or more, the client was required to obtain ratings from at least two different credit rating agencies. The 2011-amended version removed this specific dual-rating obligation and replaced it with a general requirement to obtain a rating 'in accordance with the relevant regulations' — without specifying any threshold or number of agencies. The original version was specific about two agencies for issues ≥ Rs 100 crore, while the amended version is general and regulatory-compliant, effectively removing the mandatory dual-rating clause.

regulation_a: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — original Regulation 9(f) scope of CRA activities (pre-2018 Second Amendment)
regulation_b: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — Regulation 9(f) as substituted by SEBI (CRA) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 11-09-2018
context_a: Regulation 9(f) (pre-Second Amendment 2018): a credit rating agency shall not carry out any activity other than the rating of securities offered by way of public or rights issue. Provided that nothing in these regulations shall prohibit a credit rating agency from engaging in any other activity in so far as it may be required by a financial sector regulator.
context_b: Regulation 9(f) (as substituted w.e.f. 11-09-2018): a credit rating agency shall not carry out any activity other than the rating of securities that are listed or proposed to be listed on a stock exchange recognized by the Board. Nothing contained in these regulations shall preclude a credit rating agency from carrying out any other activity as may be specified by the Board or carrying out rating of financial instruments under the purview of any other financial sector regulator or authority as may be specified by the Board.
question: Do the original and 2018-amended versions of Regulation 9(f) define the same permissible scope of rating activities for a credit rating agency?
answer: No
explanation: The original Regulation 9(f) restricted CRAs to rating only securities 'offered by way of public or rights issue' — a narrower category limited to primary market securities. The 2018-amended version expanded this to rating of securities 'that are listed or proposed to be listed on a stock exchange' — significantly broadening scope to cover secondary market listed securities as well. Additionally, the amended version changed the exception from allowing 'any activity required by a financial sector regulator' to a more specific carveout for rating of financial instruments 'under the purview of any other financial sector regulator or authority as specified by the Board.' The two versions define materially different (and contradictory) scope of permissible rating activities.

regulation_a: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — original Regulation 16(3) on withdrawal of rating (pre-2018 amendment)
regulation_b: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — Regulation 16(3) as substituted by SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 30-05-2018
context_a: Regulation 16(3) (pre-2018): A credit rating agency shall not withdraw a rating so long as the obligations under the security rated by it are outstanding, except where the company whose security is rated is wound up or merged or amalgamated with another company.
context_b: Regulation 16(3) (as substituted w.e.f. 30-05-2018): A credit rating agency shall not withdraw a rating so long as the obligations under the security rated by it are outstanding, except where the company whose security is rated is wound up or merged or amalgamated with another company, or as may be specified by the Board from time to time.
question: Do the original and 2018-amended versions of Regulation 16(3) provide the same grounds for a credit rating agency to withdraw a rating of an outstanding security?
answer: No
explanation: The original version permitted rating withdrawal only in two specific circumstances: (1) when the rated company is wound up, or (2) when it is merged or amalgamated with another company. The 2018-amended version retained these two grounds but added a third: withdrawal is also permitted 'as may be specified by the Board from time to time.' This open-ended carveout grants SEBI the authority to specify additional circumstances for rating withdrawal — an additional ground not present in the original version. The two versions are contradictory in the breadth of permissible withdrawal grounds.

regulation_a: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — definition of 'credit rating agency' under Regulation 2(h) (pre-2021 amendment)
regulation_b: SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 — definition of 'credit rating agency' under Regulation 2(h) as amended w.e.f. 03-08-2021
context_a: Regulation 2(h) (pre-2021): 'credit rating agency' means a body corporate which is engaged in, or proposes to be engaged in, the business of rating of securities offered by way of public or rights issue.
context_b: Regulation 2(h) (as amended w.e.f. 03-08-2021): 'credit rating agency' means a body corporate which is engaged in, or proposes to be engaged in, the business of rating of securities that are listed or proposed to be listed on a stock exchange recognized by the Board.
question: Do the original and 2021-amended definitions of 'credit rating agency' under Regulation 2(h) cover the same scope of rating activity?
answer: No
explanation: The original definition covered only the rating of securities 'offered by way of public or rights issue' — restricting the concept of a CRA to primary market issuances (IPOs and rights issues). The 2021-amended definition broadened this to cover securities 'that are listed or proposed to be listed on a stock exchange recognized by the Board' — encompassing both primary market issuances and existing listed securities. The original definition was limited to new issues, while the amended definition includes all listed securities (primary and secondary market). These are contradictory in scope.

context: Regulation 5(c) (original): the applicant has a minimum net worth of rupees five crores. [Substituted by SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 30-05-2018]: the applicant has a minimum net worth of rupees twenty five crore.
question: When was the minimum net worth requirement for credit rating agency applicants under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 raised, and by how much was it increased?
answer: The minimum net worth requirement was raised by the SEBI (Credit Rating Agencies) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018, with effect from 30 May 2018. The original 1999 regulations set a minimum of Rs five crore. The 2018 amendment increased this to Rs twenty-five crore — a five-fold increase of Rs twenty crore.

context: Regulation 14(g) (original): the client shall agree to obtain a rating from at least two different rating agencies for any issue of debt securities whose size is equal to or exceeds, rupees one hundred crores. [Substituted by SEBI (CRA) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2011, w.e.f. 27-12-2011]: the client shall obtain a rating for any issue of debt securities in accordance with the relevant regulations.
question: Under the original SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999, what was the mandatory dual-rating requirement for large debt issues, and when was it removed?
answer: Under the original Regulation 14(g), a client was required to obtain ratings from at least two different credit rating agencies for any debt securities issue of Rs 100 crore or more. This mandatory dual-rating requirement was removed by the SEBI (Credit Rating Agencies) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2011, with effect from 27 December 2011. The 2011 amendment replaced this specific dual-rating obligation with a general requirement to obtain a rating 'in accordance with the relevant regulations.'

context: Regulation 8(2) (as amended by 2011 Amendment): certificate of initial registration valid for 5 years. Regulation 8A (inserted by 2011 Amendment): application for permanent registration to be made 3 months before expiry of initial certificate. [Regulation 8A omitted and Regulation 8(2) substituted by the Change in Conditions of Registration of Certain Intermediaries (Amendment) Regulations, 2016 w.e.f. 08-12-2016.] Current Regulation 8(2): valid unless suspended or cancelled.
question: Describe the evolution of the certificate validity regime for credit rating agencies under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 from the original regulations through to the current position.
answer: The evolution of certificate validity for CRAs under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 went through three stages: (1) Original (1999): The original regulation prescribed a fixed validity period of three years for the certificate of registration. (2) 2011 Amendment (w.e.f. 05-07-2011): SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2011 introduced a two-stage regime — an 'initial registration' valid for five years, after which the CRA could apply for 'permanent registration' (application to be made three months before expiry of the initial certificate). (3) 2016 Amendment (w.e.f. 08-12-2016): The SEBI (Change in Conditions of Registration of Certain Intermediaries) (Amendment) Regulations, 2016 abolished both the initial/permanent registration distinction and all fixed validity periods — the certificate is now valid in perpetuity unless suspended or cancelled by SEBI.

context: Regulation 2(h) — definition of 'credit rating agency' (pre-2021): rating of securities offered by way of public or rights issue. [Amended by SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2021 w.e.f. 03-08-2021]: rating of securities that are listed or proposed to be listed on a stock exchange recognized by the Board.
question: What change did the SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2021 make to the definition of 'credit rating agency' under Regulation 2(h)?
answer: The SEBI (Credit Rating Agencies) (Amendment) Regulations, 2021 (w.e.f. 03-08-2021) substituted the definition of 'credit rating agency' under Regulation 2(h). The original definition restricted 'credit rating agencies' to entities rating securities 'offered by way of public or rights issue' — covering only primary market issuances. The 2021 amendment broadened the definition to cover rating of securities 'that are listed or proposed to be listed on a stock exchange recognized by the Board' — expanding the scope to include all listed securities and not just those offered via public or rights issues.

context: Chapter IVA (ESG Rating Providers) inserted by SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2023, w.e.f. 4.07.2023. Regulation 28C: no person shall act as an ESG rating provider unless it has obtained a certificate from the Board.
question: When was the regulatory framework for ESG rating providers introduced under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: The regulatory framework for ESG rating providers was introduced through the insertion of Chapter IVA (comprising Regulations 28A to 28I) by the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Credit Rating Agencies) (Amendment) Regulations, 2023, with effect from 4 July 2023. Prior to this amendment, SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 contained no provisions specifically governing ESG rating providers.

context: Regulation 5(l) (inserted by SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 30-05-2018): the promoter of the credit rating agency, in terms of regulation 4, has a minimum shareholding of 26% in the credit rating agency.
question: When was the 26% minimum promoter shareholding requirement for credit rating agencies introduced under SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: The requirement for a minimum promoter shareholding of 26% in a credit rating agency was introduced by the SEBI (Credit Rating Agencies) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018, with effect from 30 May 2018. Prior to this amendment, the SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 did not prescribe any minimum shareholding requirement for promoters. The 2018 amendment inserted Regulation 5(l) (eligibility criterion) and Regulation 9(e) (ongoing condition of certificate) to impose this requirement.

context: Regulation 24A (inserted by SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 30-05-2018): cross-shareholding restrictions between CRAs. A CRA shall not directly or indirectly hold 10% or more shareholding in any other CRA, or have board representation in any other CRA.
question: When were the cross-shareholding restrictions between credit rating agencies first introduced in SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999?
answer: The cross-shareholding restrictions between credit rating agencies (Regulation 24A) were first introduced by the SEBI (Credit Rating Agencies) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018, with effect from 30 May 2018. Prior to the 2018 amendment, the SEBI (CRA) Regulations, 1999 did not contain any specific prohibition on CRAs holding shares in other CRAs or shareholders of one CRA holding 10% or more in another CRA.

context: Regulation 15(1) (pre-2018): Every credit rating agency shall, during the lifetime of securities rated by it continuously monitor the rating of such securities. Regulation 15(1) (as substituted by SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 30-05-2018): Every credit rating agency shall, during the lifetime of securities rated by it continuously monitor the rating of such securities, unless the rating is withdrawn, subject to the provisions of regulation 16(3).
question: What change did the SEBI (CRA) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018 make to the continuous monitoring obligation under Regulation 15(1)?
answer: The SEBI (Credit Rating Agencies) (Amendment) Regulations, 2018 (w.e.f. 30-05-2018) added a qualifying carveout to the continuous monitoring obligation in Regulation 15(1). The original version required CRAs to continuously monitor ratings 'during the lifetime of securities rated by it' with no exception. The 2018 amendment added the phrase 'unless the rating is withdrawn, subject to the provisions of regulation 16(3)' — clarifying that the monitoring obligation ceases if and when a rating is legitimately withdrawn in accordance with the conditions specified in Regulation 16(3) (i.e., winding up, merger, or as specified by the Board).

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 3(1): No acquirer shall acquire shares or voting rights in a target company which taken together with shares or voting rights held by him and by persons acting in concert entitle them to exercise twenty-five per cent or more of the voting rights in such target company unless the acquirer makes a public announcement of an open offer.
question: Under Regulation 3(1) of SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations 2011, what voting rights threshold triggers the mandatory open offer obligation?
answer: 25% or more of the voting rights in the target company (when combined with shares held by the acquirer and persons acting in concert).

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 3(2): No acquirer who together with persons acting in concert has acquired and holds in accordance with these regulations shares or voting rights entitling them to exercise twenty-five per cent or more but less than the maximum permissible non-public shareholding, shall acquire within any financial year additional shares or voting rights entitling them to exercise more than five per cent of the voting rights, unless the acquirer makes a public announcement of an open offer.
question: An acquirer holds 30% voting rights in a target company. What is the maximum additional percentage of voting rights they can acquire in a single financial year under Regulation 3(2) without triggering an open offer?
answer: 5% of voting rights per financial year.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 4: Irrespective of acquisition or holding of shares or voting rights in a target company, no acquirer shall acquire, directly or indirectly, control over such target company unless the acquirer makes a public announcement of an open offer for acquiring shares of such target company in accordance with these regulations.
question: Can an acquirer acquire control over a target company without making a public announcement of an open offer, even if they do not acquire any new shares?
answer: No. Regulation 4 states that irrespective of acquisition or holding of shares or voting rights, no acquirer shall acquire control over a target company without making a public announcement of an open offer.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 5(2): In case of an indirect acquisition where (a) the proportionate net asset value of the target company as a percentage of the consolidated NAV, (b) the proportionate sales turnover as a percentage of consolidated sales turnover, or (c) the proportionate market capitalisation as a percentage of enterprise value of the entity being acquired is in excess of eighty per cent, such indirect acquisition shall be regarded as a direct acquisition for all purposes including timing, pricing and compliance requirements.
question: Under Regulation 5(2) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011, under what condition is an indirect acquisition treated as a direct acquisition for all purposes of the regulations?
answer: When the proportionate NAV, proportionate sales turnover, or proportionate market capitalisation of the target company exceeds 80% of the consolidated NAV, consolidated sales turnover, or enterprise value (respectively) of the entity being acquired, on the basis of the most recent audited annual financial statements.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 6(1): An acquirer who together with persons acting in concert holds shares or voting rights entitling them to exercise twenty-five per cent or more but less than the maximum permissible non-public shareholding, shall be entitled to voluntarily make a public announcement of an open offer for acquiring shares in accordance with these regulations, subject to their aggregate shareholding after completion of the open offer not exceeding the maximum permissible non-public shareholding.
question: What shareholding range qualifies an acquirer to voluntarily make a public announcement of an open offer under Regulation 6(1) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011?
answer: The acquirer must hold shares or voting rights of 25% or more but less than the maximum permissible non-public shareholding, and the aggregate shareholding after completion of the offer must not exceed the maximum permissible non-public shareholding.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 6(2): An acquirer and persons acting in concert who have made a public announcement under this regulation to acquire shares of a target company shall not be entitled to acquire any shares of the target company for a period of six months after completion of the open offer except pursuant to another voluntary open offer.
question: Under Regulation 6(2), after completing a voluntary open offer, for how long is the acquirer prohibited from acquiring further shares of the target company (other than through another voluntary open offer)?
answer: Six months after completion of the open offer.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 6A: Notwithstanding anything contained in these regulations, no person who is a wilful defaulter shall make a public announcement of an open offer for acquiring shares or enter into any transaction that would attract the obligation to make a public announcement of an open offer for acquiring shares under these regulations. Provided that this regulation shall not prohibit the wilful defaulter from making a competing offer upon any other person making an open offer for acquiring shares of the target company.
question: What does Regulation 6A of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011 prohibit, and what exception does it carve out for wilful defaulters?
answer: Regulation 6A prohibits wilful defaulters from making a public announcement of an open offer or entering into any transaction triggering an open offer obligation. However, it does not prohibit a wilful defaulter from making a competing offer when another person has made an open offer for shares of the same target company.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 7(1): The open offer for acquiring shares to be made by the acquirer and persons acting in concert under regulation 3 and regulation 4 shall be for at least twenty six per cent of total shares of the target company, as of tenth working day from the closure of the tendering period.
question: Under Regulation 7(1) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011, what is the minimum open offer size for a mandatory open offer triggered under Regulations 3 or 4?
answer: At least 26% of the total shares of the target company (as of the tenth working day from the closure of the tendering period).

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 8(8): Where the acquirer has acquired or agreed to acquire any shares or voting rights in the target company during the offer period at a price higher than the offer price, the offer price shall stand revised to the highest price paid or payable for any such acquisition. Provided that no such acquisition shall be made after the third working day prior to the commencement of the tendering period and until the expiry of the tendering period.
question: What happens to the offer price if an acquirer purchases shares of the target company during the offer period at a price higher than the declared offer price under Regulation 8(8)?
answer: The offer price automatically stands revised to the highest price paid or payable for such acquisition. However, no such acquisition is permitted after the third working day prior to the commencement of the tendering period and during the tendering period.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 8(10): Where the acquirer or persons acting in concert acquires shares of the target company during the period of twenty-six weeks after the tendering period at a price higher than the offer price under these regulations, the acquirer and persons acting in concert shall pay the difference between the highest acquisition price and the offer price, to all the shareholders whose shares were accepted in the open offer, within sixty days from the date of such acquisition.
question: Under Regulation 8(10), if an acquirer buys shares of the target company within 26 weeks after the tendering period at a price higher than the offer price, what obligation arises and within what timeframe must it be fulfilled?
answer: The acquirer must pay the difference between the highest acquisition price and the open offer price to all shareholders whose shares were accepted in the open offer, within 60 days from the date of such acquisition. The obligation does not apply to acquisitions under another open offer, the Delisting Regulations, or ordinary open market purchases not involving negotiated transactions.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 9(2): For purposes of payment by way of listed shares, the shares sought to be issued or exchanged shall conform to the following: (a) listed and frequently traded; (b) listed for at least two years preceding the date of public announcement; (c) issuer has redressed at least 95% of complaints received from investors in the preceding calendar quarter; (d) issuer has been in material compliance with listing regulations for at least two years immediately preceding the date of public announcement; (e) impact of auditors' qualifications does not exceed 5% of net profit or loss after tax for three immediately preceding financial years; (f) SEBI has not issued directions against the issuer restricting capital market access.
question: What conditions must listed shares offered as non-cash consideration in an open offer satisfy under Regulation 9(2) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011?
answer: The shares must: (a) be listed and frequently traded; (b) have been listed for at least 2 years before the date of public announcement; (c) the issuer must have redressed at least 95% of investor complaints in the preceding calendar quarter; (d) the issuer must have materially complied with listing regulations for at least 2 years preceding the public announcement; (e) impact of auditors' qualifications must not exceed 5% of net profit or loss after tax for three preceding financial years; and (f) SEBI must not have issued any direction restricting the issuer's access to capital markets or issuance of fresh shares.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 10(3): An increase in voting rights in a target company of any shareholder beyond the limit attracting an obligation to make an open offer under sub-regulation (1) of regulation 3, pursuant to buy-back of shares by the target company shall be exempt from the obligation to make an open offer provided such shareholder reduces his shareholding such that his voting rights fall to below the threshold referred to in sub-regulation (1) of regulation 3 within ninety days from the date of the closure of the said buy-back offer.
question: Under Regulation 10(3), what must a shareholder do to claim exemption from making an open offer when their voting rights exceed the threshold solely because of a buy-back by the target company?
answer: The shareholder must reduce their shareholding such that voting rights fall below the 25% threshold within 90 days from the date of closure of the buy-back offer.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 12(1): Prior to making a public announcement, the acquirer shall appoint a merchant banker registered with the Board, who is not an associate of the acquirer, as the manager to the open offer. (2) The public announcement of the open offer shall be made by the acquirer through such manager to the open offer.
question: What is the key independence requirement for the manager to the open offer under Regulation 12(1) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011?
answer: The manager to the open offer must be a SEBI-registered merchant banker who is not an associate of the acquirer.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 16(4): The Board shall give its comments on the draft letter of offer as expeditiously as possible but not later than fifteen working days of the receipt of the draft letter of offer and in the event of no comments being issued by the Board within such period, it shall be deemed that the Board does not have comments to offer. Provided that in the event the Board has sought clarifications or additional information, the period for issuance of comments shall be extended to the fifth working day from the date of receipt of satisfactory reply.
question: Under Regulation 16(4) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011, within how many working days must SEBI give its comments on the draft letter of offer, and what is the consequence if no comments are given within that period?
answer: SEBI must give its comments within 15 working days of receipt of the draft letter of offer. If no comments are issued within this period, it is deemed that SEBI does not have comments to offer. If SEBI seeks clarifications, the deadline extends to the fifth working day from receipt of a satisfactory reply.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 17(10)(e): The escrow account deposited with the bank in cash shall be released to the entire amount to the manager to the open offer, in the event of forfeiture for non-fulfillment of any of the obligations under these regulations, for distribution in the following manner, after deduction of expenses of registered market intermediaries: (i) one third to the target company; (ii) one third to the Investor Protection and Education Fund; (iii) one third distributed pro-rata among the shareholders who have accepted the open offer.
question: Under Regulation 17(10)(e), if an acquirer's escrow account is forfeited for non-fulfillment of open offer obligations, how is the forfeited amount distributed?
answer: After deduction of expenses of registered market intermediaries: one-third to the target company; one-third to the Investor Protection and Education Fund (IPEF); and one-third distributed pro-rata among shareholders who accepted the open offer.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 22(2): Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-regulation (1), subject to the acquirer depositing in the escrow account cash of an amount equal to the entire consideration payable under the open offer assuming full acceptance of the open offer, the parties to such agreement may after the expiry of twenty-one working days from the date of detailed public statement, act upon the agreement and the acquirer may complete the acquisition.
question: Under what condition and after what period may an acquirer complete the underlying acquisition before the expiry of the offer period under Regulation 22(2)?
answer: The acquirer may complete the acquisition after expiry of 21 working days from the date of the detailed public statement, provided they have deposited in the escrow account, in cash, an amount equal to the entire consideration payable under the open offer assuming full acceptance.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 29(1): Any acquirer, together with persons acting in concert with him acquiring shares or voting rights in a target company, which taken together aggregates to five per cent or more of the shares of such target company, shall disclose their aggregate shareholding and voting rights in such form as may be specified. (2) Any person who holds shares or voting rights entitling them to five per cent or more shall disclose the number held and change in shareholding or voting rights if such change exceeds two per cent of total shareholding or voting rights in the target company. (3) Such disclosures shall be made within two working days of the acquisition or disposal.
question: Under Chapter V (Disclosure provisions) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011, when must a disclosure of acquisition be made if the acquisition aggregates to 5% or more, and within what timeframe must the disclosure be filed?
answer: An acquirer whose aggregate acquisition reaches 5% or more must disclose their aggregate shareholding and voting rights. Additionally, any person already holding 5% or more must disclose any change in shareholding exceeding 2%. All such disclosures must be made within two working days of the acquisition or disposal to every stock exchange where the target company is listed and to the target company at its registered office.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 26(2): During the offer period, unless the approval of shareholders of the target company by way of a special resolution by postal ballot is obtained, the board of directors of either the target company or any of its subsidiaries shall not: (a) alienate any material assets outside ordinary course of business; (b) effect any material borrowings outside ordinary course of business; (c) issue or allot any authorised but unissued securities entitling voting rights; (d) implement any buy-back or effect capital structure changes; (e) enter into, amend or terminate material contracts outside ordinary course; (f) accelerate any contingent vesting of rights including ESOPs.
question: Under Regulation 26(2) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011, what actions is the target company's board of directors prohibited from taking during the offer period without shareholder approval by postal ballot?
answer: The board is prohibited from: (a) alienating material assets outside ordinary course of business; (b) effecting material borrowings outside ordinary course; (c) issuing unissued securities with voting rights; (d) implementing share buy-backs or changing capital structure; (e) entering into, amending or terminating material contracts outside ordinary course; and (f) accelerating any contingent vesting of rights such as ESOPs.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 17(1) Escrow table: On the first five hundred crore rupees — 25% of the consideration; On the balance consideration — additional 10% of the balance consideration.
question: An acquirer makes a mandatory open offer where the total consideration payable is Rs 1,200 crore. What is the minimum escrow amount they must create under Regulation 17(1) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011?
answer: Rs 125 crore (25% of first Rs 500 crore) + Rs 70 crore (10% of balance Rs 700 crore) = Rs 195 crore.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 3(2): No acquirer who together with persons acting in concert holds 25% or more but less than the maximum permissible non-public shareholding, shall acquire within any financial year additional shares or voting rights entitling them to exercise more than five per cent of the voting rights, unless an open offer is made.
question: An acquirer holds 28% voting rights in a listed company with 10 crore total shares outstanding. What is the maximum number of additional shares they can acquire in financial year 2024-25 without triggering a mandatory open offer under Regulation 3(2)?
answer: 5% of 10 crore = 50 lakh shares.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 7(1): The open offer for acquiring shares to be made under regulation 3 and regulation 4 shall be for at least twenty six per cent of total shares of the target company, as of tenth working day from the closure of the tendering period.
question: A listed company has 50 crore shares outstanding. An acquirer acquires shares triggering a mandatory open offer under Regulation 3(1). What is the minimum number of shares for which the open offer must be made under Regulation 7(1)?
answer: 26% of 50 crore = 13 crore shares.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 8(10): Where the acquirer acquires shares during the period of twenty-six weeks after the tendering period at a price higher than the offer price, the acquirer shall pay the difference between the highest acquisition price and the offer price to all shareholders whose shares were accepted in the open offer, within sixty days from the date of such acquisition.
question: An open offer at Rs 100 per share completed payment to shareholders on March 15, 2024. The acquirer then purchases shares in the open market at Rs 130 per share on August 20, 2024. Does Regulation 8(10) apply, and if so, by when must the difference be paid to shareholders who tendered in the open offer?
answer: 26 weeks from March 15, 2024 is approximately September 15, 2024. August 20, 2024 falls within this window, so Regulation 8(10) applies. The acquirer must pay Rs 30 per share (Rs 130 minus Rs 100) to all tendering shareholders within 60 days from August 20, 2024, i.e., by approximately October 19, 2024.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 17(4): In the event of the escrow account being created by way of a bank guarantee or by deposit of securities, the acquirer shall also ensure that at least one per cent of the total consideration payable is deposited in cash with a scheduled commercial bank as a part of the escrow account.
question: An acquirer's total consideration payable under an open offer is Rs 600 crore. Under Regulation 17(1), the required escrow is Rs 235 crore. The acquirer intends to fund the entire escrow via bank guarantee. What minimum cash amount must they additionally deposit with a scheduled commercial bank?
answer: At least 1% of total consideration payable = 1% of Rs 600 crore = Rs 6 crore must be deposited in cash.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 16(1) fee table: Up to ten crore rupees — Rs 5,00,000; More than ten crore rupees but not more than one thousand crore rupees — 0.5% of the offer size; More than one thousand crore rupees — Rs 5,00,00,000 plus 0.125% of the portion in excess of one thousand crore rupees.
question: An acquirer makes a mandatory open offer where the total consideration payable is Rs 400 crore. What is the non-refundable fee payable to SEBI under Regulation 16(1) at the time of filing the draft letter of offer?
answer: Consideration is more than Rs 10 crore but less than or equal to Rs 1,000 crore: fee = 0.5% of offer size = 0.5% × Rs 400 crore = Rs 2 crore.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 18(8): The tendering period shall start not later than twelve working days from date of receipt of comments from SEBI under Regulation 16(4) and shall remain open for ten working days.
question: SEBI issued its comments on the draft letter of offer on April 7, 2025. Assuming no intervening non-working days, what is the last permissible date on which the tendering period can commence, and when is the last date of the tendering period?
answer: The tendering period must commence no later than 12 working days from April 7, 2025 = approximately April 23, 2025 (adding 12 working days). The tendering period then remains open for 10 working days, so the last date of the tendering period = approximately May 7, 2025 (adding 10 working days from April 23).

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 7(2): The open offer made under regulation 6 (voluntary offer) shall be for acquisition of at least such number of shares as would entitle the holder thereof to exercise an additional ten per cent of the voting rights in the target company.
question: An acquirer holds 40% voting rights in a target company with 200 crore total shares. They wish to make a voluntary open offer under Regulation 6. What is the minimum number of shares for which the offer must be made under Regulation 7(2)?
answer: At least 10% additional voting rights = 10% of 200 crore total shares = 20 crore shares.

regulation_a: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 3(1) (original provision)
regulation_b: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 3(5) (inserted by 2021 Amendment)
context_a: Regulation 3(1): No acquirer shall acquire shares or voting rights in a target company which taken together entitle them to exercise twenty-five per cent or more of the voting rights in such target company unless the acquirer makes a public announcement of an open offer.
context_b: Regulation 3(5): For the purpose of this regulation, any reference to 'twenty-five per cent' in case of listed entity which has listed its specified securities on Innovators Growth Platform shall be read as 'forty-nine per cent'.
question: Do Regulation 3(1) and Regulation 3(5) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011 contradict each other about the shareholding threshold triggering a mandatory open offer?
answer: No
explanation: There is no contradiction. Regulation 3(1) establishes the general rule that the open offer threshold is 25%. Regulation 3(5), inserted by the 2021 Amendment Regulations (w.e.f. 05-05-2021), creates a carve-out specifically for entities listed on the Innovators Growth Platform (IGP), raising the threshold to 49% for such entities. Both provisions are part of the same regulation framework and operate as a general rule and a specific exception.

regulation_a: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 3(2) main provision
regulation_b: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 3(2) proviso inserted by 2020 Amendment
context_a: Regulation 3(2): No acquirer who together with persons acting in concert holds 25% or more but less than the maximum permissible non-public shareholding shall acquire within any financial year additional shares or voting rights entitling them to exercise more than five per cent of the voting rights, unless an open offer is made.
context_b: Regulation 3(2) proviso (inserted by Amendment Regulations, 2020, w.e.f. 16-06-2020): Provided that the acquisition beyond five per cent but upto ten per cent of the voting rights in the target company shall be permitted for the financial year 2020-21 only in respect of acquisition by a promoter pursuant to preferential issue of equity shares by the target company.
question: Do the main provision and the 2020 proviso of Regulation 3(2) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011 contradict each other about the annual creep acquisition limit?
answer: No
explanation: No contradiction. The main provision sets the general annual creep limit at 5% of voting rights. The 2020 proviso created a one-time, COVID-related relaxation for FY 2020-21 only, allowing promoters to acquire up to 10% (instead of 5%) via preferential issue. This was a time-limited exception, not a permanent change to the creep limit. Both provisions coexist as a general rule and a specific, time-bound exception.

regulation_a: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 7(1)
regulation_b: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 7(2)
context_a: Regulation 7(1): The open offer for acquiring shares to be made by the acquirer and persons acting in concert under regulation 3 and regulation 4 shall be for at least twenty six per cent of total shares of the target company.
context_b: Regulation 7(2): The open offer made under regulation 6 (voluntary offer) shall be for acquisition of at least such number of shares as would entitle the holder thereof to exercise an additional ten per cent of the voting rights in the target company.
question: Is there a contradiction between Regulation 7(1) and Regulation 7(2) about the minimum open offer size under SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011?
answer: No
explanation: No contradiction. Regulation 7(1) and Regulation 7(2) apply to different categories of open offers. Regulation 7(1) governs mandatory open offers triggered under Regulations 3 and 4, requiring a minimum offer size of 26% of total shares. Regulation 7(2) governs voluntary open offers under Regulation 6, requiring a minimum of 10% additional voting rights. The different sizes reflect the different policy objectives: mandatory offers must be large enough to provide meaningful exit opportunity, while voluntary offers serve strategic accumulation purposes.

regulation_a: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 22(1)
regulation_b: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 22(2)
context_a: Regulation 22(1): The acquirer shall not complete the acquisition of shares or voting rights in, or control over, the target company, whether by way of subscription to shares or a purchase of shares attracting the obligation to make an open offer, until the expiry of the offer period.
context_b: Regulation 22(2): Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-regulation (1), subject to the acquirer depositing in the escrow account cash of an amount equal to the entire consideration payable under the open offer assuming full acceptance, the parties may after the expiry of twenty-one working days from the date of detailed public statement, act upon the agreement and the acquirer may complete the acquisition.
question: Do Regulation 22(1) and Regulation 22(2) contradict each other about when an acquirer can complete the underlying acquisition transaction?
answer: No
explanation: No contradiction. Regulation 22(1) establishes the general rule prohibiting completion of the acquisition before expiry of the offer period. Regulation 22(2) contains a well-defined exception ('notwithstanding anything in sub-regulation (1)') that permits early completion subject to two conditions: (i) depositing the entire open offer consideration in cash in escrow, and (ii) waiting at least 21 working days from the date of the detailed public statement. The two provisions operate as rule and exception.

regulation_a: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 23(1)
regulation_b: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 6A
context_a: Regulation 23(1): An open offer for acquiring shares once made shall not be withdrawn except: (a) statutory approvals having been finally refused; (b) the acquirer, being a natural person, has died; (c) any condition in the underlying agreement is not met for reasons outside the acquirer's reasonable control; or (d) such circumstances as in the opinion of the Board merit withdrawal.
context_b: Regulation 6A: No person who is a wilful defaulter shall make a public announcement of an open offer. Provided that this regulation shall not prohibit the wilful defaulter from making a competing offer upon any other person making an open offer.
question: Is there a contradiction between Regulation 23(1) and Regulation 6A regarding the circumstances under which a wilful defaulter can withdraw from an open offer obligation?
answer: No
explanation: No contradiction. These regulations address different stages of the open offer process. Regulation 6A prevents a wilful defaulter from initiating an open offer in the first place. Regulation 23(1) deals with withdrawal rights after an open offer has already been made. If a wilful defaulter somehow made an open offer (e.g., before being classified as a defaulter, or via the competing offer carve-out), Regulation 23(1) would govern the limited circumstances under which that offer can be withdrawn. The two provisions operate at different stages and do not conflict.

regulation_a: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 29(1) (as substituted by Second Amendment 2021, w.e.f. 1-4-2022)
regulation_b: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 — Regulation 29(2) (as substituted by Second Amendment 2021, w.e.f. 1-4-2022)
context_a: Regulation 29(1): Any acquirer, together with persons acting in concert, acquiring shares or voting rights in a target company which taken together aggregates to five per cent or more of the shares of such target company, shall disclose their aggregate shareholding and voting rights.
context_b: Regulation 29(2): Any person who together with persons acting in concert holds shares or voting rights entitling them to five per cent or more shall disclose the change in shareholding or voting rights if such change exceeds two per cent of total shareholding or voting rights in the target company.
question: Do Regulation 29(1) and Regulation 29(2) impose contradictory or duplicative disclosure obligations on an acquirer who crosses 5% while already holding 4% and now acquires 2% more (reaching 6% total)?
answer: No
explanation: No contradiction, though both regulations may apply simultaneously. Regulation 29(1) requires disclosure when an acquisition aggregates to 5% or more (event-based trigger). Regulation 29(2) requires disclosure when a person already holding 5% or more undergoes a change exceeding 2% (change-based trigger). In the scenario described, the acquirer crosses 5% for the first time, so Regulation 29(1) applies (acquiring 2% more to reach 6% aggregates to 5% or more). Both regulations target different disclosure scenarios and are complementary, not contradictory.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 6A was inserted by the SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2016, w.e.f. 25-05-2016. Regulation 6B was inserted by the SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 11-09-2018.
question: When did SEBI introduce separate prohibitions on wilful defaulters and fugitive economic offenders making open offers under the Takeovers Regulations, and what triggered these additions?
answer: The prohibition on wilful defaulters (Regulation 6A) was inserted by the Second Amendment Regulations, 2016, effective May 25, 2016, following Reserve Bank of India guidelines on wilful defaulters. The prohibition on fugitive economic offenders (Regulation 6B) was inserted by the Second Amendment Regulations, 2018, effective September 11, 2018, following the enactment of the Fugitive Economic Offenders Act, 2018.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 18(4): Irrespective of whether a competing offer has been made, an acquirer may make upward revisions to the offer price at any time prior to the commencement of the last one working day before the commencement of the tendering period. Substituted by the SEBI (SAST) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 11-09-2018. Prior to its substitution, it read 'three working days'.
question: How was the deadline for making upward revisions to the offer price changed under Regulation 18(4) of SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011 by the 2018 amendments?
answer: Prior to the 2018 amendment, an acquirer could make upward revisions to the offer price up to three working days before the commencement of the tendering period. The Second Amendment Regulations, 2018 (effective September 11, 2018) reduced this window to one working day before the commencement of the tendering period, tightening the timeline for last-minute price revisions.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 3(5): For the purpose of this regulation, any reference to 'twenty-five per cent' in case of listed entity which has listed its specified securities on Innovators Growth Platform shall be read as 'forty-nine per cent'. Inserted by the SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) (Amendment) Regulations, 2021, w.e.f. 05-05-2021. Regulation 1(3): The word 'institutional trading platform' was also substituted to 'Innovators Growth Platform' by the same 2021 amendment.
question: When did SEBI introduce the higher 49% open offer threshold for entities listed on the Innovators Growth Platform (formerly Institutional Trading Platform), and what changes were made simultaneously?
answer: The Amendment Regulations, 2021, effective May 5, 2021, inserted Regulations 3(5) and 6(4) providing that the 25% mandatory open offer threshold reads as 49% for IGP-listed entities. Simultaneously, the same amendment renamed 'institutional trading platform' to 'Innovators Growth Platform' throughout the regulations.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 5A was substituted by the SEBI (SAST) (Third Amendment) Regulations, 2021, w.e.f. 6-12-2021. The original Regulation 5A (inserted by the SEBI (Listing of Specified Securities on Institutional Trading Platform) Regulations, 2013) read: 'The acquirer may delist the company in accordance with the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Delisting of Equity Shares) Regulations, 2009.' The new Regulation 5A provides a detailed delisting offer framework referencing the Delisting Regulations, 2021 and includes a two-attempt structure.
question: How did the SEBI Takeovers Regulations provisions on combined open offer and delisting evolve from 2013 to 2021?
answer: In 2013, a basic Regulation 5A was inserted allowing an acquirer making an open offer to also delist under the Delisting Regulations, 2009, with specific disclosure conditions. In December 2021, the Third Amendment Regulations substituted the old Regulation 5A with a comprehensive new framework referencing the updated Delisting Regulations, 2021. The new Regulation 5A introduced: (a) detailed conditions for acquirer eligibility; (b) a two-payment structure (indicative price if delisting threshold met, open offer price if not); (c) a two-attempt delisting structure; and (d) updated timelines for withdrawal announcements.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 10(1)(b)(viii): Invocation of pledge by Scheduled Commercial Banks or Public Financial Institutions as a pledgee was listed as an acquisition in the ordinary course of business exempt from open offer obligations. Regulation 29(4) proviso: the disclosure requirement on encumbrance does not apply to a scheduled commercial bank or public financial institution or a housing finance company or a systemically important non-banking financial company as pledgee. The housing finance company and SBNFC exemption was inserted by the Third Amendment Regulations, 2018, w.e.f. 31-12-2018.
question: When were housing finance companies and systemically important NBFCs added to the list of pledgees exempt from disclosure obligations under the encumbrance provisions of SEBI Takeovers Regulations, and what was the scope of the original exemption?
answer: The original exemption in Regulation 29(4) covered only scheduled commercial banks and public financial institutions acting as pledgees in ordinary business. The Third Amendment Regulations, 2018, effective December 31, 2018, expanded this to also include housing finance companies (registered with NHB, deposit-taking or with asset size of Rs 500 crore or more) and systemically important non-banking financial companies. The expansion recognised the growing role of these institutions as lenders against pledged shares.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 30 (continual disclosures) was omitted by the SEBI (SAST) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2021, w.e.f. 1-4-2022. Prior to omission, Regulation 30 required every person holding 25%+ and all promoters to disclose their aggregate shareholding as of March 31 each year within 7 working days from year-end. Regulation 29(1) was also substituted by the same amendment, shifting from requiring disclosure only by the acquirer doing the acquisition to requiring disclosure by any acquirer (together with PAC) whose aggregate acquisition reaches 5% or more.
question: What happened to the annual continual disclosure requirement for promoters and major shareholders under SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011, and when was it removed?
answer: Regulation 30, which required every person holding 25% or more of voting rights and all promoters to disclose their aggregate shareholding as of March 31 each financial year (within 7 working days from year-end), was omitted by the Second Amendment Regulations, 2021, with effect from April 1, 2022. This was part of a broader overhaul of disclosure obligations, including changes to Regulation 29. The annual continual disclosure was likely subsumed into or replaced by disclosure requirements under the Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements Regulations, 2015.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 8(2)(e) prior to 2025: price for infrequently traded shares was determined by 'the acquirer and the manager to the open offer'. Substituted by the SEBI (SAST) (Amendment) Regulations, 2025, w.e.f. 03-01-2026, to require 'an independent registered valuer'. The same amendment also substituted similar references in Regulation 8(4) and Regulation 9(5). A 9-month transition period was provided for ongoing valuation assignments.
question: What significant change did the SEBI Takeovers Amendment Regulations, 2025 make to who can conduct share valuations for open offers, and what transitional protection was provided for ongoing assignments?
answer: The 2025 Amendment Regulations, effective January 3, 2026, replaced the requirement that valuations for infrequently traded shares (Regulation 8(2)(e) and 8(4)) and consideration shares (Regulation 9(5)) be done by the acquirer and the manager to the open offer (or an independent merchant banker/chartered accountant with 10 years' experience) with the requirement for an independent registered valuer as defined under section 247 of the Companies Act, 2013. A transition period of 9 months was provided for valuation assignments already underway before the amendment came into force.

regulation: Securities and Exchange Board of India (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011
context: Regulation 31A (Regulatory Sandbox) was inserted by the SEBI (Regulatory Sandbox) (Amendment) Regulations, 2020, w.e.f. 17-04-2020. The regulation allows SEBI to exempt any person from the operation of all or any provisions of SEBI Takeovers Regulations for up to twelve months, for furthering innovation relating to testing new products, processes, services, business models in a live regulatory sandbox environment.
question: When was the regulatory sandbox exemption introduced into SEBI Takeovers Regulations 2011, and what is the maximum duration for which SEBI can grant such an exemption?
answer: The regulatory sandbox provision (Regulation 31A) was inserted by the SEBI (Regulatory Sandbox) (Amendment) Regulations, 2020, effective April 17, 2020. SEBI can grant exemptions from any provisions of the Takeovers Regulations for a period not exceeding 12 months, subject to such conditions as SEBI deems appropriate, to enable testing of new products, processes, services, or business models in a live environment.
